For the reverse, for any $x \in A$, reflexivity gives $x \in [x]$, so $x \in \bigcup [a]$. Therefore $\bigcup [a] = A$. Hence the classes form a partition. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Let $A = \{ 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 \}$. Consider the relation $R$ on $A$ defined by $aRb$ if and only if $a \equiv b \pmod 3$. Find all equivalence classes and the quotient set $A/R$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on a set $A$. Prove that for any $a, b \in A$: $aRb$ if and only if $[a] = [b]$. #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Verify $R$ is an equivalence relation. Reflexive: $a - a = 0$ divisible by 3. Symmetric and transitive are standard for modular equivalence. ✓ **Step 2 (4 marks):** Compute equivalence classes. Class of $1$ (remainder $1$): elements $\equiv 1 \pmod 3$ in $A$ are $1, 4, 7$. $$[1] = \{ 1, 4, 7 \}$$ Class of $2$ (remainder $2$): elements are $2, 5, 8$. $$[2] = \{ 2, 5, 8 \}$$ Class of $3$ (remainder $0$): elements are $3, 6$. $$[3] = \{ 3, 6 \}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Quotient set: $$A/R = \{\, \{1, 4, 7\},\; \{2, 5, 8\},\; \{3, 6\} \,\}$$ These three classes are pairwise disjoint and their union is $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8\} = A$. ✓ #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the claim formally. Claim: $\forall a, b \in A,\; aRb \iff [a] = [b]$. **Step 2 — Forward direction ($aRb \implies [a] = [b]$) (3 marks):** Assume $aRb$. We show $[a] \subseteq [b]$: if $x \in [a]$, then $(a, x) \in R$. By transitivity with $(a, b) \in R$, we get $(b, x) \in R$, so $x \in [b]$. Hence $[a] \subseteq [b]$. Symmetrically, $[b] \subseteq [a]$. Therefore $[a] = [b]$. **Step 3 — Reverse direction ($[a] = [b] \implies aRb$) (3 marks):** Assume $[a] = [b]$. By reflexivity, $a \in [a]$. Since $[a] = [b]$, we have $a \in [b]$. This means $(b, a) \in R$. By symmetry, $(a, b) \in R$, i.e., $aRb$. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Skipping the verification of reflexive/symmetric/transitive properties (loses 2–3 marks).** Always verify all three properties before computing classes, even if it seems obvious. KTU examiners specifically award marks for verification. > 2. **Forgetting reflexivity failure.** If the relation fails reflexivity for *any* element, $R$ is **not** an equivalence relation. Many students mechanically assume it is. Always check at least one element explicitly. > 3. **Listing redundant classes.** If $[1] = \{1, 4, 7\}$ and $[4] = \{1, 4, 7\}$, only one should appear in $A/R$. Listing both loses 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting the empty class in $A/R$.** A relation may have a "trivial" class like $[0] = \{0\}$; omitting such singletons loses marks. > 5. **Mixing up $R$ (relation) with $\subseteq$ (set inclusion).** The class $[a]$ is a **set**, while $R$ is a **set of ordered pairs**. Conflating them is a common KTU mistake. > 6. **Proof by example only.** In part (b), general proofs must use quantifiers ($\forall a, b$) and the three equivalence properties explicitly. Showing one example is **not** sufficient. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Equivalence class $[a]$**: $\{x \in A \mid (a, x) \in R\}$ — the set of all elements of $A$ related to $a$. - **Quotient set $A/R$**: Set of all *distinct* equivalence classes of $A$ under $R$. - **Self-membership**: $a \in [a]$ always (reflexivity of $R$). - **Class identity test**: $aRb \iff [a] = [b]$ (a critical KTU theorem). - **Disjointness**: $[a] \cap [b] \neq \emptyset \iff [a] = [b]$. - **Coverage**: $\bigcup_{a \in A} [a] = A$ — classes exhaustively cover $A$. - **Partition Theorem**: Equivalence classes of an equivalence relation form a partition, and vice versa. - **Modular example**: For $aRb \iff a \equiv b \pmod n$, the classes are $[0], [1], \ldots, [n-1]$. - **Parity example**: "Same parity" gives $\{ \text{odd} \}$ and $\{ \text{even} \}$. - **Verification order in KTU exams**: Reflexive $\rightarrow$ Symmetric $\rightarrow$ Transitive $\rightarrow$ Class computation. - **Notation to remember**: $[a]$, $\overline{a}$, $a/R$, $\pi$, $A/R$ are all commonly used in KTU papers. - **Quick intuition**: An equivalence class is like a "team" — every member is equivalent to every other member, and every element of $A$ is on exactly one team. - **Engineering applications to recall**: DFA minimization, hashing, compiler lexers, image segmentation, software testing input partitioning. - **Common errors to avoid**: Skipping property verification, listing duplicate classes in $A/R$, treating $R$ as a set of classes, and confusing $R$ with $\subseteq$. - **Singletons**: If an element is related only to itself, its class is a singleton $\{a\}$ — never omit it.

topics.title: Partial Orderings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Partial Orderings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Partial Order

Let $A$ be a non-empty set and $R$ be a binary relation on $A$. The relation $R$ is called a **Partial Order** (or **Partial Ordering**) on $A$ if it satisfies the following three axioms simultaneously:

1. **Reflexive:** $\forall\, a \in A,\;(a, a) \in R$
2. **Antisymmetric:** $\forall\, a, b \in A,\;[(a, b) \in R \land (b, a) \in R] \Rightarrow a = b$
3. **Transitive:** $\forall\, a, b, c \in A,\;[(a, b) \in R \land (b, c) \in R] \Rightarrow (a, c) \in R$

The pair $(A, R)$ is called a **Partially Ordered Set** (or **Poset**), and is sometimes written as $(A, \preceq)$ where $\preceq$ denotes the partial order relation.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board-Standard Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> A binary relation $R$ defined on a set $A$ is said to be a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. The set $A$ along with the partial order $R$ is referred to as a partially ordered set or poset.

## 1.2 Canonical Examples of Posets

| Poset $(A, R)$ | Partial Order | Engineering / Real-world Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(\mathbb{Z}, \leq)$ | Less than or equal to integers | Number line ordering |
| $(\mathbb{N}, \mid)$ | Divisibility on natural numbers | Factorisation, GCD/LCM theory |
| $(\mathcal{P}(S), \subseteq)$ | Subset inclusion on power set | Boolean algebra, digital logic gates |
| $(S, \preceq)$ Task hierarchy | Project task scheduling | PERT/CPM, topological sorting |
| $(O, \subseteq)$ Object inheritance | Class-subclass tree | OOP class hierarchy |

## 1.3 Comparability and Incomparable Elements

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Terminology — High Yield for KTU**
> - **Comparable:** Two elements $a, b \in A$ are comparable if either $(a, b) \in R$ **or** $(b, a) \in R$ (or both, in which case $a = b$ by antisymmetry).
> - **Incomparable:** Two elements $a, b \in A$ are incomparable if $(a, b) \notin R$ **and** $(b, a) \notin R$. The notation is $a \parallel b$.

## 1.4 Total Order, Chains, and Antichains

- A partial order is called a **Total Order** (or **Linear Order**) if **every** pair of distinct elements is comparable. Hence no two elements are incomparable. $(\mathbb{R}, \leq)$ is a total order.
- A **Chain** is a subset $C \subseteq A$ in which every two elements are comparable. Every chain is itself a total order.
- An **Antichain** is a subset $S \subseteq A$ in which no two distinct elements are comparable (i.e., $a \parallel b$ for all $a \neq b \in S$).

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The University Hierarchy

Think of a university's administrative structure. The **Vice-Chancellor** is at the top, then **Deans**, then **HODs**, then **Faculty**, then **Students**. The relation "is senior to" forms a partial order because:

- Every person is at their own level. → **Reflexive**
- If $A$ is senior to $B$, then $B$ cannot also be senior to $A$ (except if $A = B$). → **Antisymmetric**
- If $A$ is senior to $B$ and $B$ is senior to $C$, then $A$ is senior to $C$. → **Transitive**

However, two **Faculty members** of different departments cannot be ranked — they are **incomparable**. This is why the order is *partial* and not *total*. A partial order is exactly the mathematical formalisation of such hierarchical ranking systems used everywhere in Computer Science (DAGs, task graphs, topological sorts).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Three Axioms

### Axiom 1: Reflexivity
- **Why required:** It establishes that every element is "related to itself". Without it, an element would have no "starting point" in the order, and chains could not be well-defined.
- **How to check:** For each $a \in A$, verify $(a, a) \in R$. On an $n \times n$ relation matrix, the principal diagonal must be entirely filled with $1$s.
- **Engineering utility:** Self-loops in dependency graphs (a task depends on itself trivially).

### Axiom 2: Antisymmetry
- **Why required:** It prevents the relation from being a two-way street. If $a \leq b$ and $b \leq a$, then $a$ and $b$ must be the *same* element. This forbids "cycles" in the strict sense.
- **How to check:** For every off-diagonal cell $(i, j)$ where $1$ is present, verify that the symmetric cell $(j, i)$ contains $0$.
- **Engineering utility:** Prevents circular dependencies in build systems (e.g., Make, Maven).

### Axiom 3: Transitivity
- **Why required:** It allows transitive "shortcuts". Once you know $a \leq b$ and $b \leq c$, you can infer $a \leq c$ without needing the explicit pair.
- **How to check:** For every pair $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$, verify $(a, c) \in R$. This is the most computationally expensive check — $O(n^3)$ in the worst case.
- **Engineering utility:** Enables path compression in union-find, transitive closure (Floyd–Warshall), database query optimisation.

## 2.2 Cover Relation and Hasse Diagram

For a poset $(A, R)$:
- An element $b$ is said to **cover** $a$ (denoted $a \lessdot b$) if $a \, R \, b$, $a \neq b$, and there is **no** $c \in A$ such that $a \, R \, c$ and $c \, R \, b$ with $c \neq a, c \neq b$.
- The **Hasse Diagram** is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) drawn with the following conventions:
  1. Each element of $A$ is represented by a small circle/dot.
  2. Cover relations are drawn as straight line segments (edges).
  3. The largest elements are placed at the top; smallest at the bottom.
  4. Self-loops and arrows are omitted (reflexivity is implicit at every node).

## 2.3 Special Elements of a Poset

Let $(A, R)$ be a poset. For $S \subseteq A$:

| Element Type | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Maximal element of $A$** | $m \in A$ such that $\nexists\, x \in A$ with $m \, R \, x$ and $m \neq x$ |
| **Minimal element of $A$** | $m \in A$ such that $\nexists\, x \in A$ with $x \, R \, m$ and $m \neq x$ |
| **Greatest element of $A$** | $g \in A$ such that $\forall\, x \in A,\; x \, R \, g$ |
| **Least element of $A$** | $\ell \in A$ such that $\forall\, x \in A,\; \ell \, R \, x$ |
| **Upper bound of $S$** | $u \in A$ such that $\forall\, x \in S,\; x \, R \, u$ |
| **Lower bound of $S$** | $\ell \in A$ such that $\forall\, x \in S,\; \ell \, R \, x$ |
| **LUB / Supremum** | Least element among all upper bounds of $S$ (denoted $S^{\uparrow}$ or $\sup S$) |
| **GLB / Infimum** | Greatest element among all lower bounds of $S$ (denoted $S^{\downarrow}$ or $\inf S$) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Distinction — Examiner's Favourite Question**
> **Greatest $\Rightarrow$ Maximal**, but **Maximal $\nRightarrow$ Greatest**. The greatest element is unique (if it exists) and is *also* maximal. However, a poset may have multiple maximal elements but no greatest element (e.g., in a "V-shaped" Hasse diagram). The same duality applies to least and minimal.

## 2.4 Lattices — A Natural Extension

A poset $(L, \leq)$ is called a **Lattice** if and only if every pair of elements $a, b \in L$ has both a **least upper bound (LUB / join $a \lor b$)** and a **greatest lower bound (GLB / meet $a \land b$)** within $L$.

- **Bounded Lattice:** A lattice with both a greatest element ($1$, called *top*) and a least element ($0$, called *bottom*).
- **Distributive Lattice:** A lattice in which join distributes over meet and vice versa: $a \lor (b \land c) = (a \lor b) \land (a \lor c)$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property / Formula | Statement | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total relations on $n$-element set | $2^{n^{2}}$ | Counting problems |
| Reflexive relations on $n$-element set | $2^{n^{2} - n}$ | Counting problems |
| Symmetric relations on $n$-element set | $2^{n(n+1)/2}$ | Counting problems |
| Antisymmetric relations on $n$-element set | $2^{n} \cdot 3^{n(n-1)/2}$ | Counting problems |
| Total orders on $n$-element set | $n!$ | Counting problems |
| Partial orders on $n$-element set (Dedekind) | $D(n)$ — sequence: $1, 1, 3, 19, 219, \ldots$ | Advanced counting |
| Cardinality of power-set lattice $\vert \mathcal{P}(S) \vert$ | $2^{\vert S \vert}$ | Power set problems |
| Divisor lattice of $n$ | Number of positive divisors of $n$, denoted $d(n)$ | Number theory |
| $\text{join}(a, b)$ in divisor lattice | $\text{lcm}(a, b)$ | GCD/LCM problems |
| $\text{meet}(a, b)$ in divisor lattice | $\gcd(a, b)$ | GCD/LCM problems |
| Width of poset | $\max \vert A' \vert$ where $A'$ is any antichain | Dilworth's theorem |
| Height of poset | $\max \vert C \vert$ where $C$ is any chain | Dilworth's theorem |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Instruction scheduling uses partial orders on dependency DAGs.
- **Database Systems:** Partial orders on timestamps enable serialisability of concurrent transactions.
- **Operating Systems:** Resource allocation graph is a poset; deadlock detection uses chain/antichain analysis.
- **VLSI / Hardware:** Boolean lattice $(\{0, 1\}^n, \leq)$ underlies Karnaugh map simplification.
- **Software Engineering:** Inheritance hierarchies in OOP languages are posets (often lattices if multiple inheritance is supported).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying a Custom Relation

**Problem:** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $R = \{(1, 1),\; (1, 2),\; (2, 1),\; (2, 2),\; (1, 3),\; (3, 3),\; (2, 3),\; (3, 2)\}$. Determine if $(A, R)$ is a poset.

### Step 1: Check Reflexivity

We need $(a, a) \in R$ for all $a \in A = \{1, 2, 3\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1, 1) &\in R \quad \checkmark \\
(2, 2) &\in R \quad \checkmark \\
(3, 3) &\in R \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reflexivity holds.** All three diagonal pairs are present.

### Step 2: Check Antisymmetry

We need: if $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, a) \in R$ with $a \neq b$, then contradiction.

Off-diagonal pairs and their symmetric counterparts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1, 2) \in R \quad &\text{and} \quad (2, 1) \in R \quad \text{with } 1 \neq 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{VIOLATION!} \\
(1, 3) \in R \quad &\text{and} \quad (3, 1) \notin R \quad \checkmark \\
(2, 3) \in R \quad &\text{and} \quad (3, 2) \in R \quad \text{with } 2 \neq 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{VIOLATION!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Antisymmetry fails.** Therefore $R$ is **not** a partial order, and the verification terminates here (no need to check transitivity).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Divisibility Poset (Comprehensive)

**Problem:** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 6, 12\}$ and let $R$ be the divisibility relation, i.e., $a \, R \, b \iff a \mid b$. Verify that $(A, R)$ is a poset, draw its Hasse diagram, and find all special elements.

### Step 1: Enumerate the Relation

$$
R = \{(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (6,6), (12,12)\} \cup \{(1,2), (1,3), (1,6), (1,12), (2,6), (2,12), (3,6), (3,12), (6,12)\}
$$

This yields $\vert R \vert = 5 + 9 = 14$ ordered pairs.

### Step 2: Verify Reflexivity

Every positive integer divides itself. So for all $a \in A$, $a \mid a$. Thus $(a, a) \in R$ for all $a \in \{1, 2, 3, 6, 12\}$.

**Reflexivity holds.** ✓

### Step 3: Verify Antisymmetry

On the positive integers, $a \mid b$ and $b \mid a$ forces $a = b$. Hence no two distinct elements in $A$ can be mutually related.

**Antisymmetry holds.** ✓

### Step 4: Verify Transitivity

If $a \mid b$ and $b \mid c$, then $a \mid c$ (a fundamental theorem of divisibility). We list a few representative transitivity checks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 \mid 2) \land (2 \mid 6) &\Rightarrow (1 \mid 6) \in R \quad \checkmark \\
(2 \mid 6) \land (6 \mid 12) &\Rightarrow (2 \mid 12) \in R \quad \checkmark \\
(1 \mid 3) \land (3 \mid 12) &\Rightarrow (1 \mid 12) \in R \quad \checkmark \\
(3 \mid 6) \land (6 \mid 12) &\Rightarrow (3 \mid 12) \in R \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All such triples hold by the transitivity of $\mid$ on $\mathbb{N}$.

**Transitivity holds.** ✓

Hence $(A, \mid)$ is a **poset**.

### Step 5: Construct the Hasse Diagram (Cover Relations)

We identify pairs $(a, b)$ where $a \mid b$, $a \neq b$, and no $c \in A$ lies strictly between them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \lessdot 2 \quad (\text{no element } c \text{ with } 1 \mid c \mid 2) \\
&1 \lessdot 3 \quad (\text{no element } c \text{ with } 1 \mid c \mid 3) \\
&2 \lessdot 6 \quad (\text{no element } c \text{ with } 2 \mid c \mid 6) \\
&2 \lessdot 12 \text{? NO: } 2 \mid 6 \mid 12, \text{ so } 6 \text{ lies between} \\
&3 \lessdot 6 \quad (\text{no element } c \text{ with } 3 \mid c \mid 6) \\
&3 \lessdot 12 \text{? NO: } 3 \mid 6 \mid 12 \\
&6 \lessdot 12 \quad (\text{no element } c \text{ with } 6 \mid c \mid 12)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final cover set:** $\{1 \lessdot 2,\; 1 \lessdot 3,\; 2 \lessdot 6,\; 3 \lessdot 6,\; 6 \lessdot 12\}$.

### Step 6: Identify Special Elements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimal elements} &= \{1\} \quad (\text{nothing divides } 1 \text{ in } A \text{ except } 1 \text{ itself}) \\
\textbf{Maximal elements} &= \{12\} \quad (\text{nothing is a multiple of } 12 \text{ in } A \text{ except } 12) \\
\textbf{Least element} &= 1 \quad (\forall a \in A,\; 1 \mid a) \\
\textbf{Greatest element} &= 12 \quad (\forall a \in A,\; a \mid 12) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bounds and LUB/GLB

**Problem:** For the same poset $(A, \mid)$ with $A = \{1, 2, 3, 6, 12\}$, find the upper bounds, lower bounds, LUB, and GLB of the subset $S = \{2, 3\}$.

### Step 1: Upper Bounds of $S = \{2, 3\}$

We need elements $u \in A$ such that $2 \mid u$ and $3 \mid u$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mid 1? \; \text{No} \quad &\Rightarrow 1 \text{ is not an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 2? \; \text{Yes}, \; 3 \mid 2? \; \text{No} \quad &\Rightarrow 2 \text{ is not an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 3? \; \text{No} \quad &\Rightarrow 3 \text{ is not an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 6? \; \text{Yes}, \; 3 \mid 6? \; \text{Yes} \quad &\Rightarrow 6 \text{ IS an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 12? \; \text{Yes}, \; 3 \mid 12? \; \text{Yes} \quad &\Rightarrow 12 \text{ IS an upper bound}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Upper bounds of $S$:** $\{6, 12\}$.

### Step 2: Lower Bounds of $S = \{2, 3\}$

We need elements $\ell \in A$ such that $\ell \mid 2$ and $\ell \mid 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \mid 2 \text{ and } 1 \mid 3 \quad &\Rightarrow 1 \text{ IS a lower bound} \\
2 \mid 2 \text{ but } 2 \nmid 3 \quad &\Rightarrow 2 \text{ is NOT a lower bound} \\
3 \mid 3 \text{ but } 3 \nmid 2 \quad &\Rightarrow 3 \text{ is NOT a lower bound} \\
6 \nmid 2 \text{ and } 6 \nmid 3 \quad &\Rightarrow 6 \text{ is NOT a lower bound} \\
12 \nmid 2 \text{ and } 12 \nmid 3 \quad &\Rightarrow 12 \text{ is NOT a lower bound}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Lower bounds of $S$:** $\{1\}$.

### Step 3: LUB and GLB

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{LUB}(2, 3) &= \min\{6, 12\} = 6 = \text{lcm}(2, 3) \\
\textbf{GLB}(2, 3) &= \max\{1\} = 1 = \gcd(2, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Generalised Rule (Memorise for KTU)**
> In the divisibility lattice $(\mathbb{N}, \mid)$:
> - $\text{join}(a, b) = \text{lcm}(a, b)$
> - $\text{meet}(a, b) = \gcd(a, b)$
>
> In the power-set lattice $(\mathcal{P}(S), \subseteq)$:
> - $\text{join}(A, B) = A \cup B$
> - $\text{meet}(A, B) = A \cap B$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Relations

| Relation Type | Reflexive | Symmetric | Antisymmetric | Transitive |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Equivalence Relation | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Partial Order** | **✓** | **✗** | **✓** | **✓** |
| Total Order | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Irreflexive | ✗ | — | ✓ | — |
| Strict Partial Order | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (vacuously) | ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Subtle Point for Board Exams**
> A relation is a partial order **only** if all three of reflexivity, antisymmetry, and transitivity hold. A common student error is to test transitivity on a relation that has already failed antisymmetry. Examiners often award **partial marks** if the work *before* the first failure is correct, but a fully correct verdict is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Verification Flowchart — Is $R$ a Partial Order?

This flowchart shows the algorithmic decision procedure used by KTU examiners to grade "verify whether $R$ is a partial order" problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Given Relation R on Set A"] --> checkRef{"Is R reflexive?
For all a in A, is aRa in R?"}
    checkRef -- No --> failOut["R is NOT a partial order"]
    checkRef -- Yes --> checkAnti{"Is R antisymmetric?
For all a != b:
if aRb and bRa then contradiction?"}
    checkAnti -- No --> failOut
    checkAnti -- Yes --> checkTran{"Is R transitive?
If aRb and bRc,
then aRc must be in R?"}
    checkTran -- No --> failOut
    checkTran -- Yes --> posetOut["(A, R) is a Poset
Proceed to Hasse Diagram"]
```

## 4.2 Hasse Diagram — Divisibility Poset on $\{1, 2, 3, 6, 12\}$

The following is a top-down block-level representation of the Hasse diagram constructed in Section 3.2. (Edges are undirected lines in a Hasse diagram; arrows are conventionally omitted because upward direction is implicit.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node12["12  (Greatest, Maximal)"] --- node6
    node6["6"] --- node2["2"]
    node6 --- node3["3"]
    node2 --- node1["1  (Least, Minimal)"]
    node3 --- node1
```

**Visual interpretation:**
- Level 4 (top): $\{12\}$
- Level 3: $\{6\}$
- Level 2: $\{2, 3\}$ (incomparable pair)
- Level 1 (bottom): $\{1\}$

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Classifying a Single Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    elemA["Element x in Poset (A, R)"] --> qMax{"Does there exist y in A
such that xRy AND x != y?"}
    qMax -- No --> maxOut["x is MAXIMAL"]
    qMax -- Yes --> qGt{"For ALL y in A, is yRx?"}
    qGt -- Yes --> gtOut["x is GREATEST
(unique if exists)"]
    qGt -- No --> maxOut
    elemA --> qMin{"Does there exist y in A
such that yRx AND x != y?"}
    qMin -- No --> minOut["x is MINIMAL"]
    qMin -- Yes --> qLt{"For ALL y in A, is xRy?"}
    qLt -- Yes --> ltOut["x is LEAST
(unique if exists)"]
    qLt -- No --> minOut
```

## 4.4 Power-Set Lattice Architecture — $(\mathcal{P}(\{a, b, c\}), \subseteq)$

The Boolean lattice on a 3-element set is a classic **bounded distributive lattice** with $2^3 = 8$ elements. The architecture below is the standard KTU diagram for this structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topL["abc  (Top / 1)"] --- midL1
    topL --- midL2
    topL --- midL3
    midL1["ab"] --- lowL1
    midL1 --- lowL2
    midL2["ac"] --- lowL1
    midL2 --- lowL3
    midL3["bc"] --- lowL2
    midL3 --- lowL3
    lowL1["a"] --- botL
    lowL2["b"] --- botL
    lowL3["c"] --- botL
    botL["empty  (Bottom / 0)"]
```

> [!NOTE] **Key Properties of this Lattice**
> - For any two elements $X, Y$: $\text{join}(X, Y) = X \cup Y$ and $\text{meet}(X, Y) = X \cap B$.
> - It is a **bounded distributive lattice** (top = $\{a,b,c\}$, bottom = $\varnothing$).
> - The lattice is **self-dual** under complementation $X \mapsto X^c$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a partial order relation. State the three properties a relation must satisfy to be a partial order.**

**Model Answer:**
A binary relation $R$ defined on a non-empty set $A$ is called a **partial order** if it satisfies the following three properties for all $a, b, c \in A$:

1. **Reflexive:** $(a, a) \in R$
2. **Antisymmetric:** If $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, a) \in R$, then $a = b$
3. **Transitive:** If $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$, then $(a, c) \in R$

The set $A$ equipped with such a relation $R$ is called a **partially ordered set (poset)**. **Example:** $(\mathbb{N}, \leq)$ and $(\mathcal{P}(S), \subseteq)$ are posets.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Hasse diagram? List the rules for drawing a Hasse diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Hasse diagram** is a pictorial representation of a finite poset in which the transitive reduction of the partial order is drawn as a layered graph.

**Rules for drawing a Hasse diagram:**
1. Each element of the poset is represented by a small dot or circle.
2. If $b$ covers $a$ (i.e., $a < b$ and no $c$ exists with $a < c < b$), then draw a line segment between $a$ and $b$, placing $b$ above $a$.
3. **Reflexive loops are omitted.**
4. **Transitive edges are omitted** (only cover relations are drawn).
5. Elements are arranged in levels, with the greatest at the top and the least at the bottom.
6. The resulting graph must be a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

**Example:** The Hasse diagram of $(\{1, 2, 3\}, \leq)$ is a vertical chain: $1 - 2 - 3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives below.

---

### Question A (Alternative 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 6, 12\}$ and let $R$ be defined as: $a \, R \, b$ if and only if $a$ divides $b$. Verify that $(A, R)$ is a **partially ordered set**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Reflexivity:* For any positive integer $a$, $a \mid a$. Hence for all $a \in A$, $(a, a) \in R$. ✓

*Step 2 — Antisymmetry:* Suppose $a \mid b$ and $b \mid a$ for $a, b \in A$. Since $a, b$ are positive integers, this forces $a = b$. Hence antisymmetry holds. ✓

*Step 3 — Transitivity:* Suppose $a \mid b$ and $b \mid c$. By the fundamental property of divisibility, $a \mid c$. Hence $(a, c) \in R$. ✓

All three properties are satisfied. Hence $(A, R)$ is a poset. ∎

**(b)** Draw the **Hasse diagram** of $(A, R)$ and identify all **maximal, minimal, greatest, and least elements**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Cover relations identified by removing reflexive and transitive pairs:*
$$1 \lessdot 2, \quad 1 \lessdot 3, \quad 2 \lessdot 6, \quad 3 \lessdot 6, \quad 6 \lessdot 12$$

*Special elements:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimal elements} &= \{1\} \quad \text{(no element strictly below 1)} \\
\textbf{Maximal elements} &= \{12\} \quad \text{(no element strictly above 12)} \\
\textbf{Least element} &= 1 \quad \text{(since } \forall a \in A,\; 1 \mid a) \\
\textbf{Greatest element} &= 12 \quad \text{(since } \forall a \in A,\; a \mid 12)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Hasse Diagram (ASCII representation for KTU answer scripts):*
```
        12
        |
        6
       / \
      2   3
       \ /
        1
```

---

### Question B (Alternative 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Show that $(\mathcal{P}(\{1, 2, 3\}), \subseteq)$ is a **lattice** by explicitly computing the join and meet of any two of its elements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The set $\mathcal{P}(\{1, 2, 3\}) = \{\varnothing, \{1\}, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{1,2\}, \{1,3\}, \{2,3\}, \{1,2,3\}\}$ has $2^3 = 8$ elements.

*Step 1 — Poset verification:* $(\mathcal{P}(S), \subseteq)$ is a poset because $\subseteq$ is reflexive ($A \subseteq A$), antisymmetric ($A \subseteq B \land B \subseteq A \Rightarrow A = B$), and transitive ($A \subseteq B \land B \subseteq C \Rightarrow A \subseteq C$).

*Step 2 — Join and Meet for sample pairs:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } X, Y \in \mathcal{P}(S): \quad \text{join}(X, Y) &= X \cup Y \\
\text{meet}(X, Y) &= X \cap Y
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explicit computations:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{join}(\{1\}, \{2\}) &= \{1\} \cup \{2\} = \{1, 2\} \\
\text{meet}(\{1\}, \{2\}) &= \{1\} \cap \{2\} = \varnothing \\
\text{join}(\{1, 2\}, \{2, 3\}) &= \{1, 2\} \cup \{2, 3\} = \{1, 2, 3\} \\
\text{meet}(\{1, 2\}, \{2, 3\}) &= \{1, 2\} \cap \{2, 3\} = \{2\} \\
\text{join}(\{1\}, \{1, 2, 3\}) &= \{1, 2, 3\} \quad (\text{the top element}) \\
\text{meet}(\varnothing, \{1, 2, 3\}) &= \varnothing \quad (\text{the bottom element})
\end{aligned}
$$

Since for every $X, Y \in \mathcal{P}(\{1, 2, 3\})$, both $X \cup Y$ and $X \cap Y$ are well-defined elements of the set, **every pair has a LUB and a GLB**. Hence $(\mathcal{P}(\{1, 2, 3\}), \subseteq)$ is a **lattice**. ∎

**(b)** For the poset $(A, \mid)$ where $A = \{1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 18\}$, find the **upper bounds, lower bounds, LUB, and GLB** of the subset $S = \{6, 9\}$ (assuming $9 \in A$ is replaced — use $S = \{2, 3\}$). Find LUB and GLB of $\{2, 3\}$. Is the poset a lattice? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Upper bounds of $S = \{2, 3\}$:*

Elements $u \in A$ such that $2 \mid u$ AND $3 \mid u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mid 6 \text{ and } 3 \mid 6 &\Rightarrow 6 \text{ is an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 12 \text{ and } 3 \mid 12 &\Rightarrow 12 \text{ is an upper bound} \\
2 \mid 18 \text{ and } 3 \mid 18 &\Rightarrow 18 \text{ is an upper bound}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Upper bounds:** $\{6, 12, 18\}$.

*Step 2 — Lower bounds of $S = \{2, 3\}$:*

Elements $\ell \in A$ such that $\ell \mid 2$ AND $\ell \mid 3$:

$$
1 \mid 2 \text{ and } 1 \mid 3 \quad &\Rightarrow 1 \text{ is a lower bound}
$$

No other element in $A$ divides both $2$ and $3$. **Lower bounds:** $\{1\}$.

*Step 3 — LUB and GLB:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{LUB}(2, 3) &= \min\{6, 12, 18\} = 6 = \text{lcm}(2, 3) \\
\textbf{GLB}(2, 3) &= \max\{1\} = 1 = \gcd(2, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Lattice check:*

For **every** pair $a, b \in A$, both $\text{lcm}(a, b)$ and $\gcd(a, b)$ exist in $A$. (You can verify this for representative pairs.) Since every two-element subset has a LUB and a GLB, $(A, \mid)$ is a **lattice**. Moreover, it has a greatest element $18$ and a least element $1$, so it is a **bounded lattice**. ∎

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "Universal" in Reflexivity (–1 mark):** Students often check reflexivity for *one* element and assume it holds for all. You MUST show $(a, a) \in R$ for **every** $a \in A$ explicitly (or state "by the definition of $\mid$ on positive integers").
>
> 2. **Confusing LUB/GLB with Max/Min (–2 marks):** LUB of $\{2, 3\}$ in the divisor lattice is **6**, NOT "any upper bound". The LUB is the *least* among the upper bounds. Same for GLB.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Remove Transitive Edges in Hasse Diagrams (–2 marks):** A common error is to draw the edge $2 \to 12$ directly. This is WRONG because $2 \lessdot 6 \lessdot 12$, so $2 \to 12$ is a transitive (not cover) edge and must be omitted.
>
> 4. **Stating Greatest Element Without Uniqueness Justification (–1 mark):** The greatest element, if it exists, is **unique**. You must either prove uniqueness (using antisymmetry) or note that it is the unique element greater than all others.
>
> 5. **Treating "Empty Poset" Casually (–1 mark):** The empty set is technically a poset (vacuously), but examiners usually expect $A \neq \varnothing$. State your assumption clearly.
>
> 6. **Drawing Hasse Diagram with Arrows:** Hasse diagrams use **undirected** line segments because direction (upward) is implied. Using arrows is non-standard and may be penalised by strict examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**
>
> - [ ] A **partial order** = reflexive + antisymmetric + transitive. Memorise this trio.
> - [ ] A **poset** $(A, R)$ is the set $A$ together with a partial order $R$.
> - [ ] **Comparable** $\Leftrightarrow$ at least one of $(a, b), (b, a)$ is in $R$.
> - [ ] **Incomparable** $\Leftrightarrow$ neither is in $R$.
> - [ ] **Total order** = partial order where every pair of distinct elements is comparable.
> - [ ] **Chain** = subset where every pair is comparable.
> - [ ] **Antichain** = subset where no pair is comparable.
> - [ ] **Cover relation** $a \lessdot b$ means $a < b$ and no element lies strictly between.
> - [ ] **Hasse diagram** = DAG of cover relations, no loops, no transitive edges, no arrows.
> - [ ] **Maximal** $\neq$ **Greatest**; multiple maximal elements can exist, but at most one greatest.
> - [ ] **Minimal** $\neq$ **Least**; multiple minimal elements can exist, but at most one least.
> - [ ] **LUB** is the *least* among the *upper bounds*; **GLB** is the *greatest* among the *lower bounds*.
> - [ ] In **divisor posets**: $\text{join} = \text{lcm}$, $\text{meet} = \text{gcd}$.
> - [ ] In **power-set posets**: $\text{join} = \cup$, $\text{meet} = \cap$.
> - [ ] A **lattice** = poset where every pair has both LUB and GLB.
> - [ ] A **bounded lattice** has both a top element ($1$) and a bottom element ($0$).
> - [ ] A **distributive lattice** satisfies $a \lor (b \land c) = (a \lor b) \land (a \lor c)$.
> - [ ] Number of antisymmetric relations on an $n$-set: $\mathbf{2^{n} \cdot 3^{n(n-1)/2}}$.
> - [ ] Number of total orders on an $n$-set: $\mathbf{n!}$.
> - [ ] Dedekind numbers (partial orders on $n$-set): $1, 1, 3, 19, 219, 4231, \ldots$
> - [ ] Always check reflexivity **first**, then antisymmetry, then transitivity. Stop at the first failure.
> - [ ] Show explicit computation for at least 2–3 cover relations when constructing a Hasse diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hasse Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hasse Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hasse Diagram (Definition):**
> A **Hasse Diagram** is a mathematical graphical representation of a finite **Partially Ordered Set (Poset)**. It depicts the *covering relation* of the poset by drawing a line (edge) upward from an element $a$ to an element $b$ whenever $b$ *covers* $a$ (i.e., $a \prec b$ and there exists no element $c$ such that $a \prec c \prec b$).

Formally, if $(P, \preceq)$ is a poset, its Hasse diagram is the directed acyclic graph (DAG) drawn such that:
- Each element of $P$ is a node (vertex).
- If $a \prec b$ (i.e., $a$ is covered by $b$), then node $a$ is placed *below* node $b$, and a straight line is drawn connecting them.
- All arrows are omitted; the upward direction is *implied* by the geometry.
- The diagram respects the *transitive reduction* of the relation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Partially Ordered Set (Poset):** A set $P$ equipped with a binary relation $\preceq$ that is **reflexive**, **antisymmetric**, and **transitive**. Example: $(\mathbb{Z}, \leq)$ is a poset.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **organizational hierarchy of a college**:
- At the **bottom** sit the freshers (1st-year students).
- Above them are the 2nd-years.
- Above them are the 3rd-years and final-years.
- At the **top** sits the Principal.

Each level *covers* the level immediately below it. You do **not** draw a line from every fresher directly to the Principal (that would be the full ordering — a *transitive clutter*). You only draw lines to the *next* level of authority. This pruned, level-wise diagram is exactly a **Hasse Diagram**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Hasse"?** Named after the German mathematician **Helmut Hasse** (1898–1979). The diagram was popularized in the West by Birkhoff, but Hasse introduced the visual technique in the 1920s for lattice theory.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology and Metrics

- **Covering Relation ($a \prec b$):** $a \preceq b$, $a \neq b$, and no $c$ exists with $a \prec c \prec b$.
- **Minimal Element:** An element $m \in P$ such that no other element of $P$ lies strictly below it. Formally: $\nexists\, x \in P$ with $x \prec m$.
- **Maximal Element:** An element $M \in P$ such that no element of $P$ lies strictly above it. Formally: $\nexists\, x \in P$ with $M \prec x$.
- **Least Element (Bottom, $\bot$):** Unique element that precedes *all* others. (Need not always exist.)
- **Greatest Element (Top, $\top$):** Unique element that is preceded by *all* others. (Need not always exist.)
- **Height / Rank:** Length of the longest chain from a minimal element to the element.
- **Width:** Size of the largest antichain (set of mutually incomparable elements).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Heuristic:** In a Hasse diagram, **minimal elements are at the bottom** with no incoming edges, and **maximal elements are at the top** with no outgoing edges.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Steps: How to Construct a Hasse Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Constructing a Hasse diagram from a poset $(P, \preceq)$ is a deterministic, four-step pipeline:

**Step 1 — Draw the Digraph (Full Order).**
- Represent each element of $P$ as a node.
- For every pair $(a, b)$ with $a \preceq b$, draw a directed edge from $a$ to $b$. This includes loops (reflexivity) at every node and a massive number of edges from transitivity.

**Step 2 — Remove Reflexive Loops.**
- Discard all self-loops ($a \to a$). They contribute no structural information.

**Step 3 — Remove Transitive Edges.**
- An edge $a \to c$ is *transitive* if there exists some $b$ such that $a \to b$ and $b \to c$ both exist. Delete all such edges.
- **Why?** The transitivity condition of the poset already guarantees $a \preceq c$ even without drawing the edge.

**Step 4 — Transitive Reduction & Visual Placement.**
- The remaining edges form the **cover relation**.
- Place all minimal elements at the **bottom row**, with each covering element drawn one level higher.
- Orient implicitly upward: lower $\Rightarrow$ smaller in the order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The Hasse diagram is essentially the **transitive reduction** of the partial order — the *smallest* DAG that preserves the reachability (ordering) information. This is widely used in **task scheduling**, **version control (DAGs)**, and **hardware logic minimization**.

## 2.2 Distinguishing Boundary Cases

| Concept | Symbol | Defining Property | Existence in Poset? |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Minimal Element | $m$ | $\nexists\, x \in P$ with $x \prec m$ | **Always exists** (in finite posets) |
| Maximal Element | $M$ | $\nexists\, x \in P$ with $M \prec x$ | **Always exists** (in finite posets) |
| Least Element ($\bot$) | $0$ | $\forall x \in P,\ 0 \preceq x$ | Need not exist; **unique if present** |
| Greatest Element ($\top$) | $1$ | $\forall x \in P,\ x \preceq 1$ | Need not exist; **unique if present** |
| Lower Bound of $S$ | $\ell$ | $\ell \preceq s$ for all $s \in S$ | May be multiple |
| Upper Bound of $S$ | $u$ | $s \preceq u$ for all $s \in S$ | May be multiple |
| Infimum ($\wedge$, GLB) | $\inf S$ | Greatest among all lower bounds | Unique if exists |
| Supremum ($\vee$, LUB) | $\sup S$ | Least among all upper bounds | Unique if exists |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** *Minimal* $\neq$ *Least*. In a poset $\{a, b\}$ with $a \not\preceq b$ and $b \not\preceq a$ (incomparable), **both $a$ and $b$ are minimal**, but **no least element exists**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Formula | Mathematical Form | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Poset (POset) axioms | Reflexive, Antisymmetric, Transitive | Denoted $(P, \preceq)$ |
| Covering Relation | $a \prec b \iff a \preceq b,\ a \neq b,\ \nexists\, c : a \prec c \prec b$ | The "edge" of a Hasse diagram |
| Hasse edge count | $E \le \binom{\vert P \vert}{2}$ | Strict upper bound |
| Chain | Totally ordered subset $a_1 \prec a_2 \prec \cdots \prec a_k$ | Length = $k-1$ |
| Antichain | Subset where no two elements are comparable | Size = width |
| Dilworth's Theorem | Width $\times$ min. chain cover $= \vert P \vert$ | Used for partition theorems |
| Lattice property | $\forall\, a, b \in P,\ a \wedge b$ and $a \vee b$ both exist | A poset where every pair has LUB & GLB |
| Bounded Lattice | Lattice with least $0$ and greatest $1$ | Used in Boolean algebra |
| Complemented Lattice | $\forall\, a,\ \exists\, a' : a \wedge a' = 0,\ a \vee a' = 1$ | Foundation of Boolean Algebra |
| Distributive Lattice | $a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)$ | Boolean algebras are distributive |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design (Topological Sort):** Task dependency graphs are Hasse diagrams of partial orders.
- **Database Theory (Inclusion Dependencies):** Schema ordering visualized as Hasse diagrams.
- **Digital Electronics (Logic Gates):** The Boolean lattice $B_n$ is a Hasse diagram with $2^n$ elements, used to minimize Boolean expressions.
- **Project Management (PERT/CPM):** Activity-on-node networks are Hasse-like DAGs.
- **Operating Systems (Resource Allocation):** Partial order of resource acquisition to prevent deadlocks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **three fully worked examples** of increasing complexity, demonstrating how to construct a Hasse diagram, identify critical elements, and determine lattice properties. Every algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Divisibility Poset on $D_{12}$

**Problem:** Draw the Hasse diagram of the poset $(D_{12}, \div)$ where $D_{12} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12\}$ and $\preceq$ denotes "divides."

### Step 1: Enumerate the Poset and Its Relations

The full set of divisibility pairs (excluding reflexivity) on $D_{12}$ is:
- $1 \mid 2,\ 1 \mid 3,\ 1 \mid 4,\ 1 \mid 6,\ 1 \mid 12$
- $2 \mid 4,\ 2 \mid 6,\ 2 \mid 12$
- $3 \mid 6,\ 3 \mid 12$
- $4 \mid 12$
- $6 \mid 12$

### Step 2: Identify the Covering Relation

A pair $(a, b)$ is a **cover** if $a \mid b$ and **no element lies strictly between them**.

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \prec 2 &\iff 1 \mid 2 \text{ and no } c \in D_{12} \text{ with } 1 \mid c \mid 2 \iff 1 \prec 2 \quad \checkmark \\
1 \prec 3 &\iff 1 \mid 3 \text{ and no } c : 1 \mid c \mid 3 \iff 1 \prec 3 \quad \checkmark \\
2 \prec 4 &\iff 2 \mid 4 \text{ and no } c \in \{3, 6\} \text{ with } 2 \mid c \mid 4 \iff 2 \prec 4 \quad \checkmark \\
2 \prec 6 &\iff 2 \mid 6 \text{ and no } c \in \{4\} \text{ with } 2 \mid c \mid 6 \iff 2 \prec 6 \quad \checkmark \\
3 \prec 6 &\iff 3 \mid 6 \text{ and no } c \in \{2, 4\} \text{ with } 3 \mid c \mid 6 \iff 3 \prec 6 \quad \checkmark \\
4 \prec 12 &\iff 4 \mid 12 \text{ and no } c \in \{6\} \text{ with } 4 \mid c \mid 12 \iff 4 \prec 12 \quad \checkmark \\
6 \prec 12 &\iff 6 \mid 12 \text{ and no } c \in \{4\} \text{ with } 6 \mid c \mid 12 \iff 6 \prec 12 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Covering Pairs:** $\{(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (2,6),\ (3,6),\ (4,12),\ (6,12)\}$.

### Step 3: Identify the Structural Elements

- **Minimal elements:** Those with no divisor other than $1$ → $\mathbf{1}$.
- **Maximal elements:** Those with no multiple in $D_{12}$ → $\mathbf{12}$.
- **Least element:** $1$ precedes every element, so $\mathbf{1}$ is the least ($\bot$).
- **Greatest element:** $12$ succeeds every element, so $\mathbf{12}$ is the greatest ($\top$).
- **Is it a Lattice?** Yes — every pair $\{a, b\}$ has a GLB (their gcd) and a LUB (their lcm), both in $D_{12}$.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Power Set Poset on $\{a, b, c\}$

**Problem:** Draw the Hasse diagram of $(P(\{a,b,c\}), \subseteq)$, where $\subseteq$ is set inclusion.

### Step 1: List the Elements

The power set has $2^3 = 8$ elements:
$$P = \{\emptyset,\ \{a\},\ \{b\},\ \{c\},\ \{a,b\},\ \{a,c\},\ \{b,c\},\ \{a,b,c\}\}$$

### Step 2: Determine Covering Relations

A set $A$ is covered by $B$ iff $A \subsetneq B$ and $\vert B \setminus A \vert = 1$ (i.e., $B$ is obtained by adding exactly one element to $A$).

| Covering Edge | Reasoning |
| :---: | :--- |
| $\emptyset \prec \{a\}$ | Adding only $a$ |
| $\emptyset \prec \{b\}$ | Adding only $b$ |
| $\emptyset \prec \{c\}$ | Adding only $c$ |
| $\{a\} \prec \{a,b\}$ | Adding only $b$ |
| $\{a\} \prec \{a,c\}$ | Adding only $c$ |
| $\{b\} \prec \{a,b\}$ | Adding only $a$ |
| $\{b\} \prec \{b,c\}$ | Adding only $c$ |
| $\{c\} \prec \{a,c\}$ | Adding only $a$ |
| $\{c\} \prec \{b,c\}$ | Adding only $b$ |
| $\{a,b\} \prec \{a,b,c\}$ | Adding only $c$ |
| $\{a,c\} \prec \{a,b,c\}$ | Adding only $b$ |
| $\{b,c\} \prec \{a,b,c\}$ | Adding only $a$ |

### Step 3: Identify Structural Elements

- **Minimal:** $\emptyset$ (only one).
- **Maximal:** $\{a,b,c\}$ (only one).
- **Least:** $\emptyset = \bot$.
- **Greatest:** $\{a,b,c\} = \top$.
- **Lattice property:** The union of two sets is the LUB; the intersection is the GLB. Therefore, this is a **Boolean Lattice $B_3$**, which is also **bounded**, **complemented**, and **distributive**.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Product Order on $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$

**Problem:** Consider the poset $P = \{0, 1\} \times \{0, 1\}$ with the product (componentwise) order:
$$(a, b) \preceq (c, d) \iff a \leq c \text{ and } b \leq d.$$

### Step 1: Enumerate the Four Elements

$$P = \{(0,0),\ (0,1),\ (1,0),\ (1,1)\}$$

### Step 2: Determine the Order Relations

| Pair | $(a \leq c)$ | $(b \leq d)$ | Comparable? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(0,0) \preceq (0,1)$ | $0 \leq 0$ ✓ | $0 \leq 1$ ✓ | **Yes** |
| $(0,0) \preceq (1,0)$ | $0 \leq 1$ ✓ | $0 \leq 0$ ✓ | **Yes** |
| $(0,0) \preceq (1,1)$ | ✓ | ✓ | **Yes** |
| $(0,1) \preceq (1,1)$ | $0 \leq 1$ ✓ | $1 \leq 1$ ✓ | **Yes** |
| $(1,0) \preceq (1,1)$ | $1 \leq 1$ ✓ | $0 \leq 1$ ✓ | **Yes** |
| $(0,1)$ vs $(1,0)$ | $0 \leq 1$ ✓ | $1 \leq 0$ ✗ | **No — Incomparable** |

### Step 3: Identify Covering Pairs and Structural Properties

- **Covering edges:** $(0,0) \prec (0,1),\ (0,0) \prec (1,0),\ (0,1) \prec (1,1),\ (1,0) \prec (1,1)$.
- **Minimal elements:** $\{(0,0)\}$ — the least element.
- **Maximal elements:** $\{(1,1)\}$ — the greatest element.
- **Lattice?** Yes, every pair has a join and a meet. This is a **Boolean Lattice $B_2$** (square-shaped Hasse diagram).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Poset | $\vert P \vert$ | Minimal | Maximal | Least | Greatest | Lattice? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $D_{12}$ | 6 | $\{1\}$ | $\{12\}$ | $1$ | $12$ | ✓ |
| $P(\{a,b,c\})$ | 8 | $\{\emptyset\}$ | $\{\{a,b,c\}\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{a,b,c\}$ | ✓ |
| $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ | 4 | $\{(0,0)\}$ | $\{(1,1)\}$ | $(0,0)$ | $(1,1)$ | ✓ |
| Incomparables on $\{a,b\}$ | 2 | $\{a, b\}$ | $\{a, b\}$ | — | — | ✗ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams render the Hasse diagrams of the three worked examples. Mermaid is used here for topological/structural fidelity, since these are *graph-theoretic* objects, not physical schematics.

## 4.1 Hasse Diagram of $(D_{12}, \div)$ — Diamond Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["1"]:::bottom
    n2["2"]
    n3["3"]
    n4["4"]
    n5["6"]
    n6["12"]:::top
    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n2 --- n4
    n2 --- n5
    n3 --- n5
    n4 --- n6
    n5 --- n6
    classDef bottom fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Arrows are implied upward. So $1 \prec 2 \prec 4 \prec 12$, $1 \prec 2 \prec 6 \prec 12$, $1 \prec 3 \prec 6 \prec 12$, etc.

## 4.2 Hasse Diagram of the Boolean Lattice $B_3$ on $P(\{a,b,c\})$

```mermaid
graph TD
    nE["EMPTY"]:::bottom
    nA["A"]
    nB["B"]
    nC["C"]
    nAB["AB"]
    nAC["AC"]
    nBC["BC"]
    nABC["ABC"]:::top
    nE --- nA
    nE --- nB
    nE --- nC
    nA --- nAB
    nA --- nAC
    nB --- nAB
    nB --- nBC
    nC --- nAC
    nC --- nBC
    nAB --- nABC
    nAC --- nABC
    nBC --- nABC
    classDef bottom fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#f1faee,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class nA,nB,nC,nAB,nAC,nBC mid
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three parallel "ranks" — singletons (rank 1), pairs (rank 2), and the full set (rank 3). This is the canonical **Boolean Lattice $B_3$**, distributive and complemented.

## 4.3 Hasse Diagram of $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ — Boolean Lattice $B_2$

```mermaid
graph TD
    n00["0,0"]:::bottom
    n01["0,1"]
    n10["1,0"]
    n11["1,1"]:::top
    n00 --- n01
    n00 --- n10
    n01 --- n11
    n10 --- n11
    classDef bottom fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** A perfect square. The two middle elements $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ are **incomparable** — neither is less than the other.

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Hasse Diagram Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT: Poset with all order pairs"]:::stage1
    B["STEP 1: Remove reflexive loops"]:::stage2
    C["STEP 2: Remove transitive edges"]:::stage2
    D["STEP 3: Identify cover relation"]:::stage3
    E["OUTPUT: Hasse Diagram"]:::stage4
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    classDef stage1 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef stage2 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef stage3 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#222,color:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A 4-stage functional pipeline showing how raw poset relations are progressively reduced to the canonical Hasse form.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Q1 (3 Marks)**

**Question:** Define a **Hasse diagram**. State any two rules for drawing it.

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 8–10 lines):**

> A Hasse diagram is a visual representation of a finite partially ordered set (poset) in which each element is represented as a point, and a line segment is drawn between two points whenever one element is covered by the other (i.e., $a \prec b$ with no element between them).
>
> **Rules for drawing:**
> 1. The smallest element (or minimal elements) is placed at the **bottom**, and the largest at the **top**. The order relation proceeds from bottom to top.
> 2. **Transitive edges are removed** — if $a \prec b$ and $b \prec c$, then the edge $a \to c$ is *not* drawn because it is implied.
> 3. **Reflexive loops are omitted** — every element relates to itself by reflexivity, but these contribute no structural information.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Q2 (3 Marks)**

**Question:** Differentiate between a **maximal element** and a **greatest element** in a poset with an example.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Maximal Element | Greatest Element |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | No element of the poset is *strictly greater* than it. | It is *greater than or equal to* every other element. |
| Uniqueness | A poset can have **multiple** maximal elements. | A greatest element is **unique** if it exists. |
| Example (poset $\{1, 2, 3\}$ with divisibility) | Both $2$ and $3$ are maximal (no multiples in the set). | No greatest element exists. |
| Example (poset $D_{12}$) | $12$ is the only maximal element. | $12$ is also the greatest element. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Module 1 Choice A**

**(a)** Draw the Hasse diagram of the poset $(D_{30}, \mid)$ where $D_{30} = \{1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30\}$. Identify the maximal, minimal, least, and greatest elements. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For the same poset, determine whether it forms a **lattice**. Justify your answer. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: List the cover relations.**

$$\begin{aligned}
&1 \prec 2, \quad 1 \prec 3, \quad 1 \prec 5, \\
&2 \prec 6, \quad 2 \prec 10, \\
&3 \prec 6, \quad 3 \prec 15, \\
&5 \prec 10, \quad 5 \prec 15, \\
&6 \prec 30, \quad 10 \prec 30, \quad 15 \prec 30.
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Hasse Diagram (rendered via Mermaid).**

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["1"]:::bottom
    n2["2"]
    n3["3"]
    n5["5"]
    n6["6"]
    n10["10"]
    n15["15"]
    n30["30"]:::top
    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n1 --- n5
    n2 --- n6
    n2 --- n10
    n3 --- n6
    n3 --- n15
    n5 --- n10
    n5 --- n15
    n6 --- n30
    n10 --- n30
    n15 --- n30
    classDef bottom fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,color:#fff
```

**Step 3: Identify the structural elements.**

- **Minimal elements:** $\{1\}$ (only $1$ has no proper divisor in $D_{30}$).
- **Maximal elements:** $\{30\}$ (only $30$ has no proper multiple in $D_{30}$).
- **Least element:** $1 = \bot$ (precedes every element).
- **Greatest element:** $30 = \top$ (succeeds every element).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Test the lattice property — every pair must have a meet and a join.**

The meet of $a$ and $b$ in a divisibility poset is $\gcd(a, b)$, and the join is $\mathrm{lcm}(a, b)$. Since $D_{30}$ is *closed* under both $\gcd$ and $\mathrm{lcm}$ (any divisor of $30$ that divides two divisors of $30$ also divides $30$), both operations stay within $D_{30}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Example:} \quad \gcd(6, 10) = 2 \in D_{30}, \quad \mathrm{lcm}(6, 10) = 30 \in D_{30} \\
\text{Example:} \quad \gcd(10, 15) = 5 \in D_{30}, \quad \mathrm{lcm}(10, 15) = 30 \in D_{30}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Conclusion.**

Yes, $(D_{30}, \mid)$ is a **lattice**, because every pair of elements has a well-defined greatest lower bound and least upper bound within the set. It is also a **bounded lattice** (with $1$ and $30$) and a **distributive lattice**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Module 1 Choice B**

**(a)** Consider the poset $P = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\}$ under the divisibility relation. Draw the Hasse diagram. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Let $A = \{4, 6\}$ be a subset of this poset. Find all **upper bounds** and **lower bounds** of $A$, and determine $\sup(A)$ and $\inf(A)$ if they exist. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Cover Relations:**
$$\begin{aligned}
&1 \prec 2, \ 1 \prec 3, \\
&2 \prec 4, \ 2 \prec 6, \\
&3 \prec 6, \\
&4 \prec 8, \ 4 \prec 12, \\
&6 \prec 12, \\
&8 \prec 24, \ 12 \prec 24.
\end{aligned}$$

**Hasse Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["1"]:::bottom
    n2["2"]
    n3["3"]
    n4["4"]
    n6["6"]
    n8["8"]
    n12["12"]
    n24["24"]:::top
    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n2 --- n4
    n2 --- n6
    n3 --- n6
    n4 --- n8
    n4 --- n12
    n6 --- n12
    n8 --- n24
    n12 --- n24
    classDef bottom fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,color:#fff
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Find all lower bounds of $A = \{4, 6\}$.**

A lower bound $\ell$ must satisfy $\ell \mid 4$ and $\ell \mid 6$. The common divisors are:
$$\text{Lower bounds} = \{1, 2\}$$

**Step 2: Find all upper bounds of $A = \{4, 6\}$.**

An upper bound $u$ must satisfy $4 \mid u$ and $6 \mid u$. The common multiples within $P$ are:
$$\text{Upper bounds} = \{12, 24\}$$

**Step 3: Determine $\inf(A)$ and $\sup(A)$.**

- The **infimum** is the *greatest* lower bound: $\max\{1, 2\} = 2$. So $\inf(A) = 2$.
- The **supremum** is the *least* upper bound: $\min\{12, 24\} = 12$. So $\sup(A) = 12$.

**Step 4: Verification.**
$$\gcd(4, 6) = 2 = \inf(A) \quad \checkmark$$
$$\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6) = 12 = \sup(A) \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Marks-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to remove transitive edges** — students often draw $1 \to 12$ *and* $1 \to 4 \to 12$. Lose **2 marks** for every such duplicate transitive edge.
> 2. **Confusing "minimal" with "least"** — these are *not* synonymous. A poset may have multiple minimal elements but no least element. Examiners deduct **1 mark** per misuse.
> 3. **Drawing the Hasse diagram top-down by mistake** — by convention, smaller elements go *below*. Reversed orientation is often marked down by **2 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting reflexivity and transitivity in poset definition** — when defining a poset, *all three* axioms (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) must be stated. Omitting one costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Mistaking GLB/LUB notation** — $\inf$ vs $\sup$ and $\wedge$ vs $\vee$ — not interchangeable. Get this wrong and lose **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hasse Diagrams:**

- **Hasse Diagram = Transitive Reduction of a Poset.** Only *covering* edges are drawn.
- **Three omisssions in drawing:** (1) no reflexive loops, (2) no transitive edges, (3) no arrowheads (direction is implicit, bottom to top).
- **Minimal elements** sit at the **bottom** (no incoming edges). **Maximal** sit at the **top** (no outgoing edges).
- **Least $\ne$ Minimal**; **Greatest $\ne$ Maximal**. Least/Greatest are *unique* (if they exist); Minimal/Maximal can be *multiple*.
- **Lower bound** is *below* every element of the set; **Upper bound** is *above* every element.
- **Infimum (GLB, $\wedge$) = Greatest** among lower bounds. **Supremum (LUB, $\vee$) = Least** among upper bounds.
- **Lattice test:** Every pair of elements must have BOTH a meet and a join. Failing either one disqualifies the poset from being a lattice.
- **Boolean Lattice $B_n$** has $2^n$ elements; it is bounded, complemented, and distributive.
- **Height** of a Hasse diagram = number of levels $- 1$. **Width** = size of the largest antichain.
- **For divisibility posets:** $\inf(a, b) = \gcd(a, b)$ and $\sup(a, b) = \mathrm{lcm}(a, b)$.
- **For subset posets:** $\inf(A, B) = A \cap B$ and $\sup(A, B) = A \cup B$.
- **Dilworth's Theorem:** In any finite poset, the size of the largest antichain equals the minimum number of chains needed to cover the entire poset.
- **Standard Posets tested by KTU:** $D_n$ (divisors), $P(S)$ (power set), $\mathbb{Z}_n \times \mathbb{Z}_m$ (product order), $A \times B$ (lexicographic order).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximal and Minimal Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximal and Minimal Elements in Partially Ordered Sets (Posets)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the study of **Partially Ordered Sets (Posets)**, the concepts of *maximal* and *minimal* elements form the foundational building blocks used to analyze the structure of ordered systems in computer science, database theory, and algorithm design.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Let $(S, \preceq)$ be a **partially ordered set** (poset). An element $a \in S$ is said to be:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximal Element:** An element $a \in S$ is called a **maximal element** of the poset $(S, \preceq)$ if there exists **no** element $b \in S$ such that $a \prec b$ (i.e., $a \preceq b$ but $a \neq b$). Formally:
> $$\forall b \in S, \quad a \preceq b \implies a = b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimal Element:** An element $a \in S$ is called a **minimal element** of the poset $(S, \preceq)$ if there exists **no** element $b \in S$ such that $b \prec a$. Formally:
> $$\forall b \in S, \quad b \preceq a \implies b = a$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **mountain range** with several peaks of varying heights.
- The peaks that **cannot be climbed any higher** from directly above (because nothing rises above them) are the **maximal elements**. You could have two equal-height peaks that both qualify as maximal because neither rises above the other.
- The **valley floors** that **nothing lies below** (no further descent possible) are the **minimal elements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A maximal element is "locally supreme" — nothing in the set is strictly greater than it. However, two maximal elements can be **incomparable** (neither is greater than the other). This is the critical distinction from the *greatest* (maximum) element, which must dominate every element in the set.

A more relatable analogy is a **corporate hierarchy chart**:
- A **CEO with no supervisor** is a maximal element.
- An **intern with no subordinates** is a minimal element.
- Two VPs at the same level with different reporting branches could *both* be maximal elements simultaneously — neither is above the other in the chart.

### 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters

| Concept | Requirement | Uniqueness |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximal** | Nothing strictly greater | May be **multiple** |
| **Maximum (Greatest)** | Greater than or equal to **all** | **Unique** if it exists |
| **Minimal** | Nothing strictly less | May be **multiple** |
| **Minimum (Least)** | Less than or equal to **all** | **Unique** if it exists |

> [!NOTE]
> Every **maximum** element is a **maximal** element, but the converse is **not true**. The same logic applies symmetrically to minimum vs. minimal. This is one of the **most heavily tested distinctions** in KTU university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown

The definitions can be decomposed into three essential logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Membership Condition:** The candidate element $a$ must belong to the underlying set $S$ of the poset. Maximal and minimal elements are *intrinsic* to the set — they are picked *from* the set, not added to it.
- **Step 2 — Non-Dominance Condition (for Maximal):** There is no *strictly greater* element in $S$. The only element that satisfies $a \preceq b$ is $a$ itself. This is the *upper bound exclusivity* property.
- **Step 3 — Non-Subordination Condition (for Minimal):** There is no *strictly less* element in $S$. The only element that satisfies $b \preceq a$ is $a$ itself. This is the *lower bound exclusivity* property.

### 2.2 Existence Theorems (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 1 (Existence in Finite Posets):** Every **finite, non-empty** partially ordered set contains **at least one maximal element** and **at least one minimal element**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2 (Uniqueness of Extremes):** A poset can have **at most one** greatest (maximum) element and **at most one** least (minimum) element. The proof follows directly from antisymmetry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 3 (Infinite Posets Caveat):** Infinite posets are **not guaranteed** to have maximal or minimal elements. For example, the poset $(\mathbb{Z}, \leq)$ on the integers has no maximal or minimal element because for any integer $n$, we can find $n+1 > n$ and $n-1 < n$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Logic Sheet

| Property | Formal Condition | KTU Exam Trigger Word |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ is **Maximal** | $\nexists \, b \in S$ such that $a \prec b$ | "no element greater than $a$" |
| $a$ is **Minimal** | $\nexists \, b \in S$ such that $b \prec a$ | "no element less than $a$" |
| $a$ is **Greatest (Max)** | $\forall b \in S, \quad b \preceq a$ | "greater than or equal to **every** element" |
| $a$ is **Least (Min)** | $\forall b \in S, \quad a \preceq b$ | "less than or equal to **every** element" |
| **Finite Poset Guarantee** | At least one max & one min exist | "finite non-empty" |
| **Uniqueness of Greatest** | At most one greatest element | if exists, it is **unique** |
| **Uniqueness of Least** | At most one least element | if exists, it is **unique** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Theory:** Maximal elements appear in **Skyline Queries** — finding non-dominated records in multi-criteria databases (e.g., hotels that are not strictly worse in every category).
- **Version Control Systems (Git):** In a DAG of commits, the **HEAD commits** with no children are maximal elements; the **root commit(s)** are minimal.
- **Task Scheduling:** Identifying tasks with no prerequisites (minimal) and tasks that are final deliverables (maximal).
- **Compiler Design:** Topological sorting of dependency graphs relies on repeatedly selecting minimal elements.
- **Operating Systems:** Resource allocation hierarchies and process priority trees use these concepts to find leaves and roots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard KTU Problem

**Problem:** Let $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12\}$ with the partial order defined as divisibility "$\mid$". Find the maximal and minimal elements.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify the partial order relation. Here, $a \preceq b$ if and only if $a$ divides $b$ (i.e., $a \mid b$).

**Step 2:** Find the **maximal elements**. We need elements $a \in S$ such that no other element in $S$ is strictly divisible-divisible by $a$.

- For $1$: $1 \mid 2$, $1 \mid 3$, etc. So $1$ is **not maximal**.
- For $2$: $2 \mid 6$, $2 \mid 12$. So $2$ is **not maximal**.
- For $3$: $3 \mid 6$, $3 \mid 12$. So $3$ is **not maximal**.
- For $4$: $4 \mid 12$. So $4$ is **not maximal**.
- For $6$: $6 \mid 12$. So $6$ is **not maximal**.
- For $12$: Is there any element $b \in S$ with $12 \mid b$? Since $12$ is the largest, **no**. So $12$ is **maximal**.

**Step 3:** Find the **minimal elements**. We need elements $a \in S$ such that no other element in $S$ divides $a$.

- For $1$: Is there $b \in S$ with $b \mid 1$ and $b \neq 1$? **No** (only 1 divides 1). So $1$ is **minimal**.
- For $2$: $1 \mid 2$. So $2$ is **not minimal**.
- For $3, 4, 6, 12$: All are divisible by $1$. So none are minimal.

**Step 4:** Check for greatest/least elements.

- Is there a **greatest** element? Yes — $12$ is divisible by every element, so $12$ is also the **maximum**.
- Is there a **least** element? Yes — $1$ divides every element, so $1$ is also the **minimum**.

**Final Result:**

$$\text{Maximal} = \{12\}, \quad \text{Minimal} = \{1\}, \quad \text{Greatest} = \{12\}, \quad \text{Least} = \{1\}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multiple Maximal/Minimal (The Classic KTU Trap)

**Problem:** Consider the poset $(S, \preceq)$ where $S = \{a, b, c, d\}$ and the Hasse diagram has $a$ and $b$ as the only minimal elements, $c$ and $d$ as the only maximal elements, with $a \preceq c$, $a \preceq d$, $b \preceq c$, $b \preceq d$. Find all maximal, minimal, greatest, and least elements.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Identify Minimals:** An element is minimal if nothing lies below it.

- $a$ has nothing below it. ✓ **Minimal**.
- $b$ has nothing below it. ✓ **Minimal**.
- $c$ has $a$ and $b$ below it. ✗
- $d$ has $a$ and $b$ below it. ✗

**Step 2 — Identify Maximals:** An element is maximal if nothing lies above it.

- $c$ has nothing above it. ✓ **Maximal**.
- $d$ has nothing above it. ✓ **Maximal**.
- $a$ has $c$ and $d$ above it. ✗
- $b$ has $c$ and $d$ above it. ✗

**Step 3 — Check for Greatest Element:** Is there an element greater than or equal to *all* elements?

- $c$ is greater than or equal to $a$ and $b$, but $c$ and $d$ are **incomparable**. So $c$ is not greater than $d$. ✗
- $d$ is not greater than $c$. ✗
- **No greatest element exists.**

**Step 4 — Check for Least Element:** Is there an element less than or equal to *all* elements?

- $a$ is not less than $b$ (incomparable). ✗
- $b$ is not less than $a$. ✗
- **No least element exists.**

**Final Result:**

$$\text{Maximal} = \{c, d\}, \quad \text{Minimal} = \{a, b\}, \quad \text{Greatest} = \emptyset, \quad \text{Least} = \emptyset$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example demonstrates the **cardinal rule**: Multiple maximal/minimal elements do not imply a maximum/minimum. This is the **#1 most-asked concept** in KTU university exams.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Verification via Formal Definition

**Verification that $c$ is maximal using the formal definition:**

$$\forall b \in S, \quad c \preceq b \implies c = b$$

Checking every $b \in S = \{a, b, c, d\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = a &: c \preceq a \text{ is FALSE} \quad (\text{so implication holds trivially}) \\
b = b &: c \preceq b \text{ is FALSE} \quad (\text{so implication holds trivially}) \\
b = c &: c \preceq c \text{ is TRUE and } c = c \quad (\text{condition satisfied}) \\
b = d &: c \preceq d \text{ is FALSE} \quad (\text{so implication holds trivially})
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the implication holds for all $b \in S$, the element $c$ is verified to be **maximal**. By symmetric reasoning applied to the Hasse diagram, $d$ is also maximal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hasse Diagram of Example 1 (Divisibility Poset on $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12\}$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["1 (Minimal / Least)"]:::minNode
    node2["2"]:::midNode
    node3["3"]:::midNode
    node4["4"]:::midNode
    node6["6"]:::midNode
    node12["12 (Maximal / Greatest)"]:::maxNode

    node1 --- node2
    node1 --- node3
    node1 --- node4
    node2 --- node6
    node3 --- node6
    node2 --- node12
    node3 --- node12
    node4 --- node12
    node6 --- node12

    classDef minNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef midNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef maxNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Green node (1):** The unique minimal AND least element — it sits at the bottom with no predecessors.
- **Pink node (12):** The unique maximal AND greatest element — it sits at the top with no successors.
- **Orange nodes (2, 3, 4, 6):** Intermediate elements that are neither maximal nor minimal.

---

### 4.2 Hasse Diagram of Example 2 (Multiple Maximals & Minimals)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["a (Minimal)"]:::minNode
    nodeB["b (Minimal)"]:::minNode
    nodeC["c (Maximal)"]:::maxNode
    nodeD["d (Maximal)"]:::maxNode

    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD

    classDef minNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef maxNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Two minimal elements** (a, b) sit at the bottom — neither is below the other.
- **Two maximal elements** (c, d) sit at the top — neither is above the other.
- **No greatest or least element** exists, because no single element dominates or is dominated by all others.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Identifying Extremes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Given element a in poset S"]:::startNode
    q1{"Is there any b in S with a ≺ b?"}:::decision
    isMax["a is MAXIMAL"]:::resultNode
    notMax["a is NOT maximal"]:::resultNode
    q2{"Is a greater than or equal to EVERY b in S?"}:::decision
    isGreatest["a is GREATEST (maximum)"]:::resultNode
    notGreatest["No greatest element via a"]:::resultNode

    startA --> q1
    q1 -- "No" --> isMax
    q1 -- "Yes" --> notMax
    isMax --> q2
    q2 -- "Yes" --> isGreatest
    q2 -- "No" --> notGreatest

    classDef startNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Flowchart Logic:** First check the *maximal* condition (local non-domination). Only if that passes, then check the stricter *greatest* condition (global domination).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

Define a **minimal element** in a partially ordered set. How is it different from a **least element**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An element $a \in S$ in a poset $(S, \preceq)$ is called a **minimal element** if there is no element $b \in S$ such that $b \prec a$ (i.e., $b$ is strictly less than $a$).
>
> **Difference:** A *minimal element* only requires that **no element is less than it**. A *least element* (minimum) is stricter — it requires $a \preceq b$ for **every** $b \in S$. Every least element is minimal, but a poset can have multiple minimal elements with no least element.

*(Expected marking: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the distinction with example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

Is it possible for a partially ordered set to have **more than one maximal element**? Justify your answer with an example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Yes**, a poset can have multiple maximal elements. Two elements are both maximal if neither is comparable to the other and no element lies above either of them.
>
> **Example:** In the poset $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with the relation $\preceq = \{(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (1, 3)\}$ (where 1 is below both 2 and 3, but 2 and 3 are incomparable), both $2$ and $3$ are maximal elements since no element in $S$ is strictly greater than $2$ or strictly greater than $3$.

*(Expected marking: 1 mark for "Yes", 1 mark for justification, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** Define a **maximal element** and a **greatest element** in a poset. Show with a concrete example that a poset may have a maximal element without having a greatest element. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the poset $(S, \mid)$ where $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\}$ and "$\mid$" denotes divisibility. Draw the Hasse diagram and find all maximal, minimal, greatest, and least elements. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Maximal Element (2 Marks):**
An element $a \in S$ in a poset $(S, \preceq)$ is **maximal** if there is no $b \in S$ such that $a \prec b$. Formally:

$$\forall b \in S, \quad a \preceq b \implies a = b$$

**Definition of Greatest Element (2 Marks):**
An element $a \in S$ is the **greatest element** (or maximum) if $a \preceq b$ for every $b \in S$:

$$\forall b \in S, \quad a \preceq b$$

Note that the greatest element, if it exists, must be **comparable to every element** of the poset, whereas a maximal element only needs to have no element strictly above it.

**Counter-Example (3 Marks):**

Consider the poset $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with the partial order $\preceq = \{(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (1,3)\}$. The Hasse diagram is:

$$1 \quad \text{(bottom)} \qquad 2 \quad \text{and} \quad 3 \quad \text{(top, incomparable)}$$

- $2$ is **maximal** because no element in $S$ satisfies $2 \prec b$.
- $3$ is **maximal** because no element in $S$ satisfies $3 \prec b$.
- However, there is **no greatest element** because $2$ and $3$ are incomparable — neither is greater than the other. So the poset has maximal elements but no greatest element.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Hasse Diagram Construction (3 Marks):**

The poset is $(S, \mid)$ where $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\}$.

Step 1: List the divisibility chains from $1$ (the bottom):
- $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 8 \to 24$
- $1 \to 2 \to 6 \to 12 \to 24$
- $1 \to 3 \to 6 \to 12 \to 24$
- $1 \to 3 \to 12 \to 24$

The Hasse diagram is:

```
              24
           /  |  \
          8  12   (12 also comes from 4, 6, 3)
           \  |  /
            4   6
             \ / 
              2
              |
              1
```

(More precisely: 1 is at the bottom, 2 and 3 are above it, 4 and 6 are above 2, 8 is above 4, 12 is above 4, 6, and 3, and 24 is above 8 and 12.)

**Identification of Extremes (4 Marks):**

- **Minimal Elements:** $1$ is the only minimal element (nothing divides 1 except 1 itself).
- **Maximal Elements:** $24$ is the only maximal element (no element in $S$ is a multiple of 24 other than 24 itself).
- **Greatest Element:** $24$ is the **greatest** because $24$ is divisible by every element in $S$ (i.e., for all $x \in S$, $x \mid 24$).
- **Least Element:** $1$ is the **least** because $1$ divides every element in $S$.

**Final Answer:**

$$\text{Minimal} = \text{Least} = \{1\}, \quad \text{Maximal} = \text{Greatest} = \{24\}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Style]

**(a)** State and prove the theorem that a **finite non-empty poset** has at least one maximal and at least one minimal element. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the poset whose Hasse diagram is given below:

```
            e
           / \
          c   d
           \ /
            b
            |
            a
```

Identify all **maximal elements**, **minimal elements**, **greatest element** (if any), and **least element** (if any). Justify each answer. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Theorem Statement (2 Marks):**
> Every finite, non-empty partially ordered set $(S, \preceq)$ has **at least one maximal element** and **at least one minimal element**.

**Proof (5 Marks):**

We prove the existence of a maximal element. The proof for the minimal element is symmetric.

Let $S$ be a non-empty finite poset. Pick any element $a_1 \in S$.

**Case 1:** $a_1$ is maximal. Then we are done.

**Case 2:** $a_1$ is not maximal. Then by definition, there exists $a_2 \in S$ such that $a_1 \prec a_2$.

Now examine $a_2$:

**Case 2a:** $a_2$ is maximal. Done.

**Case 2b:** $a_2$ is not maximal. Then there exists $a_3 \in S$ with $a_2 \prec a_3$.

Continuing this process, we construct a chain:

$$a_1 \prec a_2 \prec a_3 \prec a_4 \prec \cdots$$

Since $S$ is **finite**, this strictly increasing chain cannot continue indefinitely (by the well-ordering property and the pigeonhole principle). The process must terminate at some element $a_k \in S$ such that $a_k$ has no element strictly greater than it in $S$. Therefore, $a_k$ is a **maximal element** of $S$.

**Symmetry Argument (Implicit):** To prove the existence of a minimal element, apply the above argument to the reverse order $\succeq$ on $S$, or repeat the same construction looking for strictly *decreasing* chains.

Hence, every finite non-empty poset has at least one maximal and at least one minimal element. $\blacksquare$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Reading the Hasse Diagram (1 Mark):**
The set is $S = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ with covering relations:
- $a \prec b$
- $b \prec c$
- $b \prec d$
- $c \prec e$
- $d \prec e$

**Finding Minimal Elements (2 Marks):**
An element is minimal if no element lies below it.
- $a$: Nothing is below $a$. ✓ **Minimal**
- $b, c, d, e$: All have elements below them. ✗

$$\text{Minimal} = \{a\}$$

**Finding Maximal Elements (2 Marks):**
An element is maximal if no element lies above it.
- $e$: Nothing is above $e$. ✓ **Maximal**
- $a, b, c, d$: All have elements above them. ✗

$$\text{Maximal} = \{e\}$$

**Greatest Element (1 Mark):**
