$e$ is the only candidate. We verify: is $e$ comparable to every element and greater than all? Yes — $a \preceq b \preceq c \preceq e$ and $a \preceq b \preceq d \preceq e$. So $e \succeq x$ for all $x \in S$.

$$\text{Greatest Element} = e$$

**Least Element (1 Mark):**
$a$ is the only candidate. Is $a$ less than or equal to every element? Yes — $a \preceq b$, $a \preceq c$, $a \preceq d$, $a \preceq e$.

$$\text{Least Element} = a$$

**Final Summary:**

$$\text{Minimal} = \text{Least} = \{a\}, \quad \text{Maximal} = \text{Greatest} = \{e\}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Maximal with Maximum (2-mark deduction each occurrence):** Students frequently claim that a poset "cannot have two maximums" but then incorrectly list multiple maximal elements as multiple maximums. Always distinguish carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the "strict" inequality in the definition (1-mark deduction):** Writing $a \preceq b$ instead of $a \prec b$ in the maximal definition will be marked wrong. The relation must be **strict**, not just weak.
> 3. **Ignoring the "finite" condition in existence theorems (2-mark deduction):** The theorem that every poset has a maximal/minimal element applies **only to finite posets**. On infinite posets like $(\mathbb{Z}, \leq)$, maximal elements may not exist.
> 4. **Not drawing Hasse diagrams (1-2 mark deduction):** KTU examiners explicitly award partial marks for the Hasse diagram. Always include a clear diagram for divisibility-based problems.
> 5. **Skipping justification in example problems (1-mark deduction):** Merely stating "the maximal elements are X" without showing *why* (which other elements are above/below) will cost you a mark. Always include the verification step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maximal Element:** No element in $S$ is **strictly greater** than it. **Multiple** maximal elements are allowed.
- **Minimal Element:** No element in $S$ is **strictly less** than it. **Multiple** minimal elements are allowed.
- **Greatest Element (Maximum):** Greater than or equal to **every** element in $S$. **Unique** if it exists.
- **Least Element (Minimum):** Less than or equal to **every** element in $S$. **Unique** if it exists.
- **Hierarchy of Strength:** Greatest $\Rightarrow$ Maximal, and Least $\Rightarrow$ Minimal. The converses are **not true**.
- **Existence Guarantee:** Every **finite, non-empty** poset has $\geq 1$ maximal and $\geq 1$ minimal element.
- **Infinite Poset Caution:** Infinite posets are **not guaranteed** to have maximal or minimal elements (e.g., $\mathbb{Z}$ under $\leq$).
- **Uniqueness Rule:** A poset can have **at most one** greatest and **at most one** least element (by antisymmetry).
- **Hasse Diagram Reading:** Elements at the **top with no upward edges** are maximal; elements at the **bottom with no downward edges** are minimal.
- **Common Posets Tested:** Divisibility on integers, subset inclusion on power sets, divisibility on $\{1, 2, ..., n\}$ factor sets.
- **Engineering Applications:** Git commit DAGs, database Skyline queries, topological sorting, task dependency graphs, priority queues.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lattices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lattices

# Lattices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A **Lattice** is a foundational algebraic structure in Discrete Mathematics that unifies the order-theoretic notion of *partial order* with the algebraic operations of *meet* and *join*. Lattices form the backbone of Boolean Algebra, formal logic, database theory, and computer program semantics.

### Formal Definition (Order-Theoretic)

A **Lattice** is a partially ordered set (poset) $(L, \preceq)$ in which **every two-element subset** $\{a, b\} \subseteq L$ possesses:
- a **Least Upper Bound (LUB)** — called the **Join** — denoted $a \vee b$
- a **Greatest Lower Bound (GLB)** — called the **Meet** — denoted $a \wedge b$

### Formal Definition (Algebraic)

Equivalently, a **Lattice** is an algebraic structure $(L, \wedge, \vee)$ consisting of a non-empty set $L$ and two binary operations $\wedge$ (meet) and $\vee$ (join) satisfying the following axioms for all $a, b, c \in L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(L1) Idempotent Laws:} \quad & a \wedge a = a, \quad a \vee a = a \\
\text{(L2) Commutative Laws:} \quad & a \wedge b = b \wedge a, \quad a \vee b = b \vee a \\
\text{(L3) Associative Laws:} \quad & (a \wedge b) \wedge c = a \wedge (b \wedge c), \quad (a \vee b) \vee c = a \vee (b \vee c) \\
\text{(L4) Absorption Laws:} \quad & a \vee (a \wedge b) = a, \quad a \wedge (a \vee b) = a
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Both definitions are **equivalent**. The order-theoretic definition arises from partial order, while the algebraic definition abstracts those bounds into binary operations. Examiners love asking students to *convert* from one form to the other.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **family tree** that has been simplified into a vertical chart. Every pair of cousins has exactly one common parent (their **least common ancestor**) and one common descendant (**greatest shared descendant**). The family tree is a *lattice*. In a company hierarchy, two employees will always share **one immediate common boss** (the *meet*) and **one immediate common subordinate** (the *join*). That is a lattice too — it is any structure where comparison and combination always resolve neatly.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A lattice is a structured hierarchy where you can always determine a "highest common combination" and a "lowest common agreement" between any two elements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Why and How

A lattice is built from the interaction of **two binary operations** with five *core identities*. Let us dissect each:

| Identity | Why It Matters | Practical Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Idempotence** | An element combined with itself does not change. | A redundant filter applied twice gives the same filter. |
| **Commutativity** | The order of operands does not change the result. | Parallel AND/OR gates can be swapped. |
| **Associativity** | Grouping does not matter — essential for tree/hardware reductions. | Parenthesization in expression trees becomes irrelevant. |
| **Absorption** | The dominating element "absorbs" the weaker one. | A true condition (OR) overrides a false branch. |
| **Partial Order** | Ensures LUB and GLB are well-defined. | Guarantees determinism in compilers and logic circuits. |

## 2.2 Deriving Lattice Properties from the Axioms

The dual of any identity in a lattice is also an identity. That is, swapping $\wedge$ and $\vee$ throughout a theorem yields another valid theorem. This is the **Principle of Duality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duality Principle:** Every lattice-theoretic statement remains true if $\wedge$ and $\vee$ are interchanged everywhere (and the partial order $\preceq$ is replaced by its dual $\succeq$).

### Key Derived Properties

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(P1) } & a \wedge (a \vee b) = a \wedge a = a \\
\text{(P2) } & a \vee (a \wedge b) = a \vee a = a \\
\text{(P3) } & a \wedge (a \vee b) = a \quad \text{(absorption again)} \\
\text{(P4) } & a \preceq b \iff a \wedge b = a \iff a \vee b = b
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Important Sub-Classes of Lattices (KTU High-Yield)

| Lattice Type | Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bounded Lattice** | A lattice with a universal least element $\mathbf{0}$ and a universal greatest element $\mathbf{1}$. | Switching algebra, digital logic circuits. |
| **Complete Lattice** | Every subset (not just pairs) has both a LUB and a GLB. | Denotational semantics of programs, fixed-point theory. |
| **Sublattice** | A non-empty subset closed under both $\wedge$ and $\vee$. | Sub-circuits, modular logic blocks. |
| **Distributive Lattice** | $a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)$ and its dual. | Boolean algebra, set theory. |
| **Complemented Lattice** | For every $a$, there exists $b$ with $a \wedge b = \mathbf{0}$ and $a \vee b = \mathbf{1}$. | Boolean Algebra — every element has a complement. |
| **Modular Lattice** | If $a \preceq c$, then $a \vee (b \wedge c) = (a \vee b) \wedge c$. | Projective geometry, submodule lattices. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Identity | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Meet definition | $a \wedge b = \text{GLB}(a,b)$ | Largest element $\le$ both $a$ and $b$. |
| Join definition | $a \vee b = \text{LUB}(a,b)$ | Smallest element $\ge$ both $a$ and $b$. |
| Idempotence | $a \wedge a = a$, $\ a \vee a = a$ | Simplification law. |
| Commutativity | $a \wedge b = b \wedge a$, $\ a \vee b = b \vee a$ | Order-independence. |
| Associativity | $(a \wedge b)\wedge c = a\wedge(b \wedge c)$ | Parenthesization freedom. |
| Absorption | $a \vee (a \wedge b) = a$, $\ a\wedge (a\vee b)=a$ | Reduction law. |
| Bounded complement | $a \wedge a' = \mathbf{0}$, $\ a \vee a' = \mathbf{1}$ | Boolean inversion. |
| Distributive law | $a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)$ | Lattice is distributive. |
| Modular law | If $a \preceq c$, then $a \vee (b \wedge c) = (a \vee b) \wedge c$ | Lattice is modular. |
| Isomorphism condition | Bijection preserving $\wedge$ and $\vee$ | Structural equivalence of lattices. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap (KTU):** Students often confuse $\wedge$ (logical AND / meet) with $\cap$ (set intersection) and $\vee$ (logical OR / join) with $\cup$ (set union). In set-theoretic lattices, they are equivalent — but in abstract lattices, $\wedge$ and $\vee$ are purely algebraic symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Logic Design:** Every Boolean expression lives inside a distributive complemented lattice.
- **Compiler Optimization:** Data-flow lattices (e.g., the lattice of available expressions) enable monotonic analyses.
- **Databases:** The set of all subsets of attributes closed under a closure operator forms a complete lattice — central to *functional dependency theory*.
- **Cryptography & Access Control:** Lattices of permissions underpin role-based access models.
- **Formal Methods & Semantics:** The denotational semantics of programs is built on **complete lattices** (Scott domains) to ensure existence of fixed points.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof: Equivalence of Order-Theoretic and Algebraic Definitions

**Statement:** If $(L, \preceq)$ is a poset where every pair has a LUB and GLB, then defining $a \wedge b = \text{GLB}(a,b)$ and $a \vee b = \text{LUB}(a,b)$ yields a lattice $(L, \wedge, \vee)$ satisfying L1–L4.

### Step 1 — Idempotence (L1)

The greatest lower bound of $\{a, a\}$ is $a$ itself (since $a \le a$ trivially). Hence:

$$a \wedge a = a$$

Similarly, the least upper bound of $\{a, a\}$ is $a$:

$$a \vee a = a$$

### Step 2 — Commutativity (L2)

The set $\{a, b\}$ equals the set $\{b, a\}$, so its LUB and GLB are identical:

$$a \wedge b = \text{GLB}(a,b) = \text{GLB}(b,a) = b \wedge a$$

$$a \vee b = \text{LUB}(a,b) = \text{LUB}(b,a) = b \vee a$$

### Step 3 — Associativity (L3)

We must show $(a \wedge b) \wedge c = a \wedge (b \wedge c)$. Let $x = (a \wedge b) \wedge c$. By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x \preceq a \wedge b \preceq a \quad &\Rightarrow \quad x \preceq a \\
x \preceq a \wedge b \preceq b \quad &\Rightarrow \quad x \preceq b \\
x \preceq c &\Rightarrow \quad x \preceq b \wedge c
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $x \preceq a$ and $x \preceq (b \wedge c)$, so $x$ is a lower bound of $\{a, b \wedge c\}$.

Now suppose $y$ is *any* lower bound of $\{a, b \wedge c\}$. Then $y \preceq a$ and $y \preceq (b \wedge c)$, the latter giving $y \preceq b$ and $y \preceq c$. So $y \preceq (a \wedge b)$ and $y \preceq c$, hence $y \preceq (a \wedge b) \wedge c = x$. Therefore $x$ is the *greatest* lower bound of $\{a, b \wedge c\}$, which means:

$$(a \wedge b) \wedge c = a \wedge (b \wedge c)$$

The dual argument proves associativity of $\vee$.

### Step 4 — Absorption (L4)

We prove $a \wedge (a \vee b) = a$. Observe:

$$a \preceq a \preceq a \vee b \quad \Rightarrow \quad a \preceq a \vee b$$

Since $a \le a$ trivially, $a$ is a lower bound of $\{a, a \vee b\}$. Now suppose $y$ is any lower bound of this set. Then $y \preceq a$, so $y$ cannot exceed $a$. Therefore $a$ is the *greatest* lower bound:

$$a \wedge (a \vee b) = a$$

The dual argument gives $a \vee (a \wedge b) = a$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Proof: In a Bounded Lattice, Complements Are Unique

**Statement:** In a bounded distributive lattice $(L, \wedge, \vee, \mathbf{0}, \mathbf{1})$, if $x$ has a complement $\bar{x}$, then $\bar{x}$ is **unique**.

### Step 1 — Suppose Two Complements Exist

Assume $y$ and $z$ are both complements of $x$. Then by definition of complement:

$$x \wedge y = \mathbf{0}, \quad x \vee y = \mathbf{1}$$

$$x \wedge z = \mathbf{0}, \quad x \vee z = \mathbf{1}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $y$ via Absorption and Distributivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= y \wedge \mathbf{1} \quad \text{(identity law)} \\
  &= y \wedge (x \vee z) \quad \text{(since } x \vee z = \mathbf{1}\text{)} \\
  &= (y \wedge x) \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(distributivity)} \\
  &= \mathbf{0} \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(since } x \wedge y = \mathbf{0}\text{)} \\
  &= (x \wedge z) \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(identity, } \mathbf{0} = x \wedge z\text{)} \\
  &= (x \vee y) \wedge z \quad \text{(distributivity)} \\
  &= \mathbf{1} \wedge z \quad \text{(since } x \vee y = \mathbf{1}\text{)} \\
  &= z \quad \text{(identity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $y = z$, and the complement is unique. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Recognising Lattice Types

| Given Structure | Test | If TRUE | If FALSE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Has $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$? | Check existence | **Bounded** | Not bounded |
| Every subset has LUB/GLB? | Check all subsets | **Complete** | Only finitely complete |
| Subset closed under $\wedge, \vee$? | Test closure | **Sublattice** | Just a sub-poset |
| Distributive law holds? | Test both directions | **Distributive Lattice** | Non-distributive |
| Every element has complement? | Check existence | **Complemented Lattice** | Not complemented |
| Modular law holds? | Test the conditional | **Modular Lattice** | Non-modular |
| Diamond $M_3$ or Pentagon $N_5$ absent? | Check forbidden substructures | **Distributive** | **Non-distributive** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Birkhoff's Representation Theorem:** A finite lattice is distributive **iff** it contains neither the **diamond $M_3$** nor the **pentagon $N_5$** as a sublattice. This is one of the highest-weight results in KTU lattices module.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Verifying a Structure is a Lattice

Let $L = \{a, b, c\}$ with Hasse diagram: $a$ at bottom, $b$ and $c$ above $a$, with $b$ and $c$ **incomparable** and no top element.

> Test 1: Is $L$ a lattice?

We need to find the meet and join of every pair.

| Pair | Meet ($\wedge$) | Join ($\vee$) |
|---|---|---|
| $(a, a)$ | $a$ | $a$ |
| $(b, b)$ | $b$ | $b$ |
| $(c, c)$ | $c$ | $c$ |
| $(a, b)$ | $a$ | $b$ |
| $(a, c)$ | $a$ | $c$ |
| $(b, c)$ | $a$ (only common lower bound) | **None** (no common upper bound!) |

Since $b \vee c$ does not exist, this structure is **NOT a lattice**. It is merely a poset.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A poset is *not automatically* a lattice. You must verify that **every** pair has both a meet AND a join. Incomparable top elements often break the lattice property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hasse Diagram: A Standard 5-Element Lattice

This is the classic **diamond lattice** $M_3$ — a non-distributive lattice central to Birkhoff's theorem.

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["1 (Top)"]
    n2["a"]
    n3["b"]
    n4["c"]
    n5["0 (Bottom)"]
    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n1 --- n4
    n2 --- n5
    n3 --- n5
    n4 --- n5
```

> *Each edge represents the covering relation. The bottom $\mathbf{0}$ has $\mathbf{0} \preceq a, b, c \preceq \mathbf{1}$. Notice $a \wedge b = a \wedge c = b \wedge c = \mathbf{0}$, but $a \vee b = a \vee c = b \vee c = \mathbf{1}$. This lattice is NOT distributive.*

## 4.2 Hasse Diagram: The Pentagon $N_5$ (Forbidden Sublattice)

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["1"]
    p2["a"]
    p3["b"]
    p4["c"]
    p5["0"]
    p1 --- p2
    p2 --- p3
    p3 --- p4
    p4 --- p5
    p1 --- p5
```

> *The pentagon $N_5$ is the other forbidden sublattice that prevents a lattice from being distributive. A lattice is distributive **iff** it contains neither $M_3$ nor $N_5$.*

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Lattice Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Elements a, b in L"] --> B{Is L a Lattice?}
    B -- Yes --> C["Compute Meet: a wedge b = GLB"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Compute Join: a vee b = LUB"]
    C --> E{Sublattice?}
    D --> F{Bounded?}
    E -- Yes --> G["Verify Closure under both ops"]
    F -- Yes --> H["Check 0 and 1 exist"]
    H --> I{Distributive?}
    I -- Yes --> J["Complements unique"]
    I -- No --> K["Check for M3 or N5"]
    K -- Absent --> L["Distributive Lattice"]
    K -- Present --> M["Non-distributive Lattice"]
    B -- No --> N["Just a Poset - Reject"]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Lattice Properties

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Lattice"]
    L2["Bounded Lattice"]
    L3["Complete Lattice"]
    L4["Distributive Lattice"]
    L5["Boolean Algebra"]
    L6["Complemented Lattice"]
    L1 --> L2
    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L4
    L2 --> L6
    L4 --> L5
    L6 --> L5
```

> *Each arrow denotes "is a specialisation of." Every Boolean Algebra is a complemented distributive bounded lattice, but the converse fails.*

## 4.5 Meet-Join Computation Flow (Order-Theoretic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Pick elements a, b in L"]
    S --> M1["Find all x in L with x leq a and x leq b"]
    M1 --> M2["Among those, pick the greatest: this is a wedge b"]
    S --> J1["Find all y in L with a leq y and b leq y"]
    J1 --> J2["Among those, pick the least: this is a vee b"]
    M2 --> OUT["Output Meet and Join"]
    J2 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Lattice. Show that the set $S = \{1, 2, 3, 6\}$ under the partial order of divisibility forms a lattice.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *lattice* is a poset in which every pair of elements has a greatest lower bound (meet) and a least upper bound (join).

Consider $S = \{1, 2, 3, 6\}$ ordered by divisibility. The Hasse diagram is:

```
    6
   / \
  2   3
   \ /
    1
```

Meets and joins:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \wedge 2 = 1, \quad 1 \vee 2 = 2 \\
&1 \wedge 3 = 1, \quad 1 \vee 3 = 3 \\
&1 \wedge 6 = 1, \quad 1 \vee 6 = 6 \\
&2 \wedge 3 = 1, \quad 2 \vee 3 = 6 \\
&2 \wedge 6 = 2, \quad 2 \vee 6 = 6 \\
&3 \wedge 6 = 3, \quad 3 \vee 6 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Every pair has both a meet and a join, hence $(S, \mid)$ is a **lattice**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the principle of duality in lattices.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Principle of Duality** states that every statement (theorem, identity, or property) about a lattice remains true if we:
- interchange $\wedge$ (meet) and $\vee$ (join) throughout the statement.
- replace the partial order $\preceq$ by its dual $\succeq$.

For example, the absorption law $a \wedge (a \vee b) = a$ has the dual $a \vee (a \wedge b) = a$, which is also valid. This principle holds because the axioms L1–L4 are self-dual — each axiom's dual is also an axiom.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **bounded lattice**. With a suitable example, show that a bounded lattice can have zero, one, or more than one complement for a given element.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove that in a bounded distributive lattice, the complement of an element (if it exists) is **unique**.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **bounded lattice** is a lattice $(L, \wedge, \vee)$ that contains a **least element** $\mathbf{0}$ and a **greatest element** $\mathbf{1}$, satisfying:

$$\mathbf{0} \preceq a \preceq \mathbf{1} \quad \text{for all } a \in L$$

**Examples illustrating complement counts:**

| Lattice | Element | Complements | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L = \{0, a, 1\}$ (chain) | $a$ | $a$ (self-complement), $0$, $1$ — but only if all satisfy both rules | varies |
| 4-element chain $\{0 \prec a \prec b \prec 1\}$ | $a$ | only $b$ (since $a \wedge b = a \ne 0$ would fail) | 0 or 1 |
| Power set $\mathcal{P}(\{1,2\})$ | $\{1\}$ | $\{2\}$ only | 1 |

**Demonstration that count is not fixed:**

Consider the lattice $\{0, a, b, 1\}$ with $0 \prec a \prec 1$ and $0 \prec b \prec 1$, but $a, b$ incomparable (diamond $M_2$).

- Complements of $a$: we need $x$ with $a \wedge x = 0$ and $a \vee x = 1$. Only $b$ works.
- Complements of $0$: only $1$ works.
- So in this diamond, every element has **exactly one** complement.

But in a non-distributive bounded lattice, complements can be **multiple or zero**. E.g., the lattice $\{0, a, b, c, 1\}$ in pentagon shape may have no complement for $b$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Theorem:** In a bounded distributive lattice, the complement of an element (if it exists) is unique.

**Proof:**

Suppose $x \in L$ has two complements $y$ and $z$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&x \wedge y = \mathbf{0}, \quad x \vee y = \mathbf{1} \\
&x \wedge z = \mathbf{0}, \quad x \vee z = \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $y$ as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= y \wedge \mathbf{1} \quad \text{(identity)} \\
  &= y \wedge (x \vee z) \quad \text{(since } x \vee z = \mathbf{1}\text{)} \\
  &= (y \wedge x) \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(distributivity)} \\
  &= \mathbf{0} \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(since } x \wedge y = \mathbf{0}\text{)} \\
  &= (x \wedge z) \vee (y \wedge z) \quad \text{(identity, } \mathbf{0} = x \wedge z\text{)} \\
  &= (x \vee y) \wedge z \quad \text{(distributivity)} \\
  &= \mathbf{1} \wedge z \quad \text{(since } x \vee y = \mathbf{1}\text{)} \\
  &= z \quad \text{(identity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $y = z$, proving uniqueness. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` — *Internal Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **distributive lattice**. Prove that every chain is a distributive lattice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the lattice $L = \{0, a, b, c, 1\}$ with partial order defined by Hasse diagram: $0 \prec a \prec 1$, $0 \prec b \prec 1$, $0 \prec c \prec 1$, with $a, b, c$ pairwise incomparable. Determine whether $L$ is a lattice, and if so, classify it (bounded, distributive, complemented).

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **distributive lattice** is a lattice $(L, \wedge, \vee)$ satisfying the **distributive laws** for all $a, b, c \in L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a \wedge (b \vee c) &= (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c) \\
a \vee (b \wedge c) &= (a \vee b) \wedge (a \vee c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theorem:** Every chain (totally ordered set) is a distributive lattice.

**Proof:**

Let $(C, \le)$ be a chain. For any $a, b, c \in C$, assume WLOG that $a \le b \le c$ (relabelling if necessary since chains are totally ordered).

**Case 1:** The dual distributive law $a \vee (b \wedge c) = (a \vee b) \wedge (a \vee c)$ holds in any lattice by the absorption-and-meet argument; but we verify directly here.

Since $a \le c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b \wedge c &= \min(b, c) = b \\
a \vee b &= \max(a, b) = b \\
a \vee c &= c
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= a \wedge (b \wedge c) = a \wedge b = a \\
\text{RHS} &= (a \wedge b) \wedge (a \wedge c) = a \wedge a = a
\end{aligned}
$$

Both sides equal $a$, so the distributive law holds. The dual case follows by symmetry. Hence every chain is a distributive lattice. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Is $L$ a lattice?**

For every pair of elements, we must verify existence of meet and join.

Since $a, b, c$ are pairwise incomparable and all lie above $0$ and below $1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&a \wedge b = 0, \quad a \vee b = 1 \\
&a \wedge c = 0, \quad a \vee c = 1 \\
&b \wedge c = 0, \quad b \vee c = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

All pairs have meet and join, so $L$ **is a lattice** (the *diamond $M_3$*).

**Step 2 — Bounded?**

Yes — $\mathbf{0} = 0$ and $\mathbf{1} = 1$ both exist. So $L$ is a **bounded lattice**.

**Step 3 — Distributive?**

Test $a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= a \wedge 1 = a \\
\text{RHS} &= 0 \vee 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $a \ne 0$, the distributive law **fails**. Therefore $L$ is **NOT distributive**.

**Step 4 — Complemented?**

For each element, we seek a complement $x$ with $a \wedge x = 0$ and $a \vee x = 1$:

- Complement of $0$: only $1$ works. ✓
- Complement of $1$: only $0$ works. ✓
- Complement of $a$: any element incomparable with $a$ that joins to $1$ and meets at $0$. Both $b$ and $c$ satisfy this. So $a$ has **two** complements. ✓
- Similarly $b$ and $c$ each have two complements.

Thus $L$ is **complemented**, but **not uniquely** complemented. This confirms $L$ is **not** a Boolean algebra (since Boolean algebras require unique complements via the bounded-distributive theorem).

**Final classification:** $L$ is a **bounded, complemented, but NON-distributive** lattice (specifically the diamond $M_3$). $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing lattice with poset:** Many students call *any* partially ordered set a lattice. Always verify LUB and GLB for *every* pair.
> 2. **Ignoring the bounded-distributive-uniqueness link:** Students forget that the uniqueness proof of complements **requires distributivity**. Without it, complements may be multiple (as in $M_3$).
> 3. **Misdrawing Hasse diagrams:** Direction of edges matters — upward represents $\preceq$. Forgetting to include $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting dual statements:** The dual of every lattice identity is also valid. Examiners love testing this.
> 5. **Wrong symbol usage:** Using $\cap, \cup$ in *abstract* lattice questions instead of $\wedge, \vee$ is considered incorrect notation in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lattice** = poset where every pair has a **LUB** and **GLB**, equivalently an algebra satisfying **idempotence**, **commutativity**, **associativity**, and **absorption**.
- **Meet** $a \wedge b$ = greatest lower bound; **Join** $a \vee b$ = least upper bound.
- **Principle of Duality:** Swap $\wedge \leftrightarrow \vee$ (and $\preceq \leftrightarrow \succeq$) — statement remains valid.
- **Bounded Lattice** $\Leftrightarrow$ has both $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$.
- **Complete Lattice** $\Leftrightarrow$ every subset (not just pairs) has LUB and GLB.
- **Distributive Lattice** $\Leftrightarrow$ both distributive laws hold; equivalent to having neither $M_3$ (diamond) nor $N_5$ (pentagon) as a sublattice.
- **Complement** $a'$ exists in a bounded lattice if $a \wedge a' = \mathbf{0}$ and $a \vee a' = \mathbf{1}$.
- **Uniqueness of complements** is guaranteed in bounded **distributive** lattices only.
- **Every chain is a distributive lattice.**
- **Every Boolean Algebra is a complemented bounded distributive lattice — but not conversely.**
- **Sublattice** must be closed under **both** $\wedge$ and $\vee$, not just under the partial order.
- **Isomorphic lattices** preserve meet and join via a bijection; structural equivalence is testable.
- **Hasse diagrams** are the standard KTU visual tool for lattices — practice drawing them from partial orders.
- **Forbidden structures:** $M_3$ and $N_5$ — remember to spot them to test distributivity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Logic and Proofs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mathematical Logic and Proofs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Mathematical Logic? **Mathematical Logic** is the formal study of *reasoning* using symbolic notation. It provides a rigorous framework to express statements, combine them using logical operators, and deduce new truths from existing ones without ambiguity. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this forms the bedrock of Discrete Mathematics and is foundational to algorithm design, digital circuits, artificial intelligence, and software verification. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A **proposition** (or statement) is a declarative sentence that is unambiguously either **True (T)** or **False (F)**, but never both and never neither. The discipline that studies how propositions are combined and manipulated to form valid arguments is called **Propositional Logic** or **Propositional Calculus**. ### 1.2 Classification of Sentences | Sentence Type | Example | Logical Status | |---|---|---| | Declarative (Factual) | "Kerala is a state in India." | **Proposition** (True) | | Declarative (False) | "2 + 2 = 5" | **Proposition** (False) | | Question | "What is 2 + 2?" | **Not a Proposition** | | Command | "Close the door." | **Not a Proposition** | | Paradox | "This sentence is false." | **Not a Proposition** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Switch" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a proposition as a Light Switch.** > - A proposition is like a single light switch — it has exactly **two stable states**: ON (True, **1**) or OFF (False, **0**). It is never "half-on." > - A **logical connective** (AND, OR, NOT, etc.) is like a *combination* of switches wired together. For example, two switches in **series** represent AND — the bulb glows only when *both* are ON. > - A **truth table** is the *wiring diagram* that tells you, for every possible switch position, whether the final bulb (the compound statement) will glow. > - A **tautology** is a circuit where the bulb is *always* glowing regardless of switch positions — it is "hard-wired" to be ON. > - A **contradiction** is a circuit that is *never* glowing — permanently OFF. ### 1.4 Atomic vs Compound Propositions - **Atomic Proposition (Atom):** A single, indivisible declarative statement. Denoted by lowercase letters $p, q, r, \dots$ Example: $p$: "It is raining." - **Compound Proposition:** Formed by combining two or more atoms using **logical connectives**. Example: $p \land q$: "It is raining AND I have an umbrella." ### 1.5 The Five (Plus Two) Fundamental Logical Connectives These are the "operators" of logic — they take truth values as input and produce a truth value as output. | Symbol | Name | Pronounced | Function | |---|---|---|---| | $\neg$ | Negation | NOT | Flips truth value | | $\land$ | Conjunction | AND | True only if both are True | | $\lor$ | Disjunction | OR | False only if both are False | | $\oplus$ | Exclusive OR | XOR | True if exactly one is True | | $\rightarrow$ | Implication | IF-THEN | False only when T $\rightarrow$ F | | $\leftrightarrow$ | Biconditional | IFF | True if both have same truth value | | $\uparrow$ | NAND | NOT-AND | False only when both True | | $\downarrow$ | NOR | NOT-OR | True only when both False | > [!NOTE] > **Precedence Order (highest to lowest):** $\neg > \land > \lor > \rightarrow > \leftrightarrow$ > Parentheses **must** be used to override this order whenever there is ambiguity. ### 1.6 Truth Table — The Master Tool A **truth table** enumerates every possible combination of truth values for the atomic propositions and computes the resulting truth value of the compound proposition. For $n$ atomic variables, there are exactly $2^n$ rows. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Any question involving more than **2 atomic variables** (i.e., 3 atoms = 8 rows) is extremely time-consuming. Examiners usually cap truth tables at **2 atoms** for 3-mark questions and **3 atoms** for full 7-mark derivations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Connective **Negation ($\neg p$):** The unary operator that inverts truth. - Logical reading: "It is NOT the case that $p$." - Truth table: T $\rightarrow$ F ; F $\rightarrow$ T. **Conjunction ($p \land q$):** The logical AND. - Logical reading: "$p$ AND $q$." Both must be true for the whole to be true. - Common English analogue: "but", "although", "while" (though grammatically different). **Disjunction ($p \lor q$):** The inclusive OR. - Logical reading: "$p$ OR $q$ (or both)." - The "exclusive OR" ($\oplus$) is a separate operator. **Implication ($p \rightarrow q$):** The conditional — the *most important* and *most abused* in KTU exams. - $p$ is called the **hypothesis** (antecedent, premise). - $q$ is called the **conclusion** (consequent). - Truth table is famously counter-intuitive: the only case where $p \rightarrow q$ is FALSE is when $p$ is True and $q$ is False. All other cases yield True. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Vacuously True" Principle:** > When $p$ is False, $p \rightarrow q$ is automatically True, *regardless* of $q$. This is called a **vacuously true** statement. KTU questions frequently test this: e.g., "If the moon is made of cheese, then 5 > 10" is a TRUE statement in logic. **Biconditional ($p \leftrightarrow q$):** - True precisely when $p$ and $q$ have the *same* truth value. - Equivalent to $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$. ### 2.2 Tautology, Contradiction, and Contingency - **Tautology ($\top$):** A compound proposition that is **always True** for every row of the truth table. Example: $p \lor \neg p$ (Law of Excluded Middle). - **Contradiction ($\bot$):** A compound proposition that is **always False**. Example: $p \land \neg p$ (Law of Non-Contradiction). - **Contingency:** A compound proposition that is True in *some* rows and False in *others* — neither always-true nor always-false. ### 2.3 Logical Equivalence Two compound propositions $A$ and $B$ are **logically equivalent**, written $A \equiv B$, if and only if they have **identical truth values in every row** of their combined truth table. > [!NOTE] > **Equivalence Symbol Alert:** KTU textbooks use $\equiv$ or $\Leftrightarrow$ for logical equivalence. Do NOT confuse $\Leftrightarrow$ (biconditional — a connective with two propositions as inputs) with $\equiv$ (equivalence — a *meta-statement* about two propositions). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Equivalence Laws > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the table below — every row is a direct or indirect source of KTU examination questions under "Prove that $A \equiv B$" or "Show using logical equivalences that..." | Law Name | Equivalence Identity | |---|---| | Identity Laws | $p \land \top \equiv p$ ; $p \lor \bot \equiv p$ | | Domination Laws | $p \lor \top \equiv \top$ ; $p \land \bot \equiv \bot$ | | Idempotent Laws | $p \lor p \equiv p$ ; $p \land p \equiv p$ | | Double Negation | $\neg(\neg p) \equiv p$ | | Commutative Laws | $p \land q \equiv q \land p$ ; $p \lor q \equiv q \lor p$ | | Associative Laws | $(p \land q) \land r \equiv p \land (q \land r)$ | | Distributive Laws | $p \land (q \lor r) \equiv (p \land q) \lor (p \land r)$ | | De Morgan's Laws (HIGH YIELD) | $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$ ; $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$ | | Absorption Laws | $p \lor (p \land q) \equiv p$ ; $p \land (p \lor q) \equiv p$ | | Negation Laws | $p \lor \neg p \equiv \top$ ; $p \land \neg p \equiv \bot$ | | Implication Elimination | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$ | | Contrapositive | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ | | Biconditional | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$ | ### 2.5 Predicates and Quantifiers (First-Order Logic) When the truth of a proposition depends on a **variable**, we use a **predicate** $P(x)$. Example: $P(x)$: "$x$ is even." The variable $x$ is called the **subject** and $P$ is the **predicate**. **Universal Quantifier ($\forall$):** "For all" / "For every." - $\forall x \, P(x)$ means $P(x)$ is true for *every* value of $x$ in the domain. - Negation: $\neg(\forall x \, P(x)) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$. **Existential Quantifier ($\exists$):** "There exists" / "For some." - $\exists x \, P(x)$ means $P(x)$ is true for *at least one* $x$ in the domain. - Negation: $\neg(\exists x \, P(x)) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **De Morgan's Laws for Quantifiers (HIGH YIELD):** > $$\neg(\forall x \, P(x)) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$ > $$\neg(\exists x \, P(x)) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$ > Memorize the rule: **Negation flips the quantifier AND negates the predicate.** ### 2.6 Rules of Inference A **rule of inference** is a template for constructing a *valid argument* — a list of premises followed by a conclusion that must be true whenever the premises are true. | Rule | Form | KTU Tag | |---|---|---| | Modus Ponens | $p, \; p \rightarrow q \;\;\therefore\;\; q$ | Most tested | | Modus Tollens | $\neg q, \; p \rightarrow q \;\;\therefore\;\; \neg p$ | Very tested | | Hypothetical Syllogism | $p \rightarrow q, \; q \rightarrow r \;\;\therefore\;\; p \rightarrow r$ | Frequently tested | | Disjunctive Syllogism | $p \lor q, \; \neg p \;\;\therefore\;\; q$ | Frequently tested | | Addition | $p \;\;\therefore\;\; p \lor q$ | Used in proofs | | Simplification | $p \land q \;\;\therefore\;\; p$ | Used in proofs | | Resolution | $(p \lor q), \; (\neg p \lor r) \;\;\therefore\;\; q \lor r$ | Used in AI/Logic Programming | ### 2.7 Methods of Proof The four classical proof techniques tested by KTU: 1. **Direct Proof:** Assume premises, apply definitions/laws, derive conclusion step-by-step. 2. **Proof by Contraposition:** Prove $p \rightarrow q$ by instead proving $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$. 3. **Proof by Contradiction:** Assume $\neg(\text{conclusion})$, add to premises, derive a contradiction ($\bot$). Hence the negation is false, so the conclusion must hold. 4. **Proof by Cases:** Split the proof into exhaustive cases based on a dichotomy (e.g., $n$ is even / $n$ is odd) and prove each case separately. ### 2.8 Engineering Utility - **Digital Logic Design:** Every gate (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR) is a physical implementation of a logical connective. Truth tables are used to design and verify combinational circuits. - **Software Verification:** Formal methods use propositional and predicate logic to prove that a program satisfies its specification (used in avionics, medical devices, railway signalling). - **AI and Knowledge Representation:** Expert systems and rule-based engines use inference rules (especially resolution) to derive new facts. - **Database Query Languages:** SQL's WHERE clauses are essentially first-order logic predicates with quantifiers. - **Algorithm Correctness:** Loop invariants, pre-conditions, and post-conditions are formalized using logical predicates. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Truth Table Construction (3 Atoms) **Problem:** Construct the truth table for the compound proposition: $$(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg q \lor r)$$ There are $n = 3$ atomic variables, so we need exactly $2^3 = 8$ rows. | $p$ | $q$ | $r$ | $\neg q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $\neg q \lor r$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg q \lor r)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | T | T | T | F | T | T | **T** | | T | T | F | F | T | F | **F** | | T | F | T | T | F | T | **F** | | T | F | F | T | F | T | **F** | | F | T | T | F | T | T | **T** | | F | T | F | F | T | F | **F** | | F | F | T | T | T | T | **T** | | F | F | F | T | T | T | **T** | > [!NOTE] > **Column-by-Column Strategy:** > 1. List all atomic inputs in the standard T-T-T-T-F-F-F-F order (Gray code pattern not required at KTU). > 2. Compute intermediate columns from smallest to largest connective. > 3. The final column is the answer. > The result above is a **contingency** (mixed T and F) — so it is neither a tautology nor a contradiction. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving Logical Equivalence Using Algebra **Problem:** Show that $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$ using logical equivalences. We begin with the LHS and transform it step-by-step until we reach the RHS. Each line must cite the law being applied. $$ \begin{aligned} \neg(p \rightarrow q) &\equiv \neg(\neg p \lor q) & &\text{(Implication Elimination: } p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q\text{)} \\ &\equiv \neg(\neg p) \land \neg q & &\text{(De Morgan's Law applied to the negation)} \\ &\equiv p \land \neg q & &\text{(Double Negation Law)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Converse Implication Logic:** This result is so important it has a name: $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$. It says that an implication is FALSE exactly when the hypothesis holds but the conclusion does not — the *only* False case of the implication. KTU loves to test this. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving Contrapositive Equivalence **Problem:** Prove that $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg q \rightarrow \neg p$. $$ \begin{aligned} p \rightarrow q &\equiv \neg p \lor q & &\text{(Implication Elimination)} \\ &\equiv q \lor \neg p & &\text{(Commutative Law)} \\ &\equiv \neg\neg q \lor \neg p & &\text{(Double Negation)} \\ &\equiv \neg q \rightarrow \neg p & &\text{(Implication Elimination in reverse)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Valid Argument Using Rules of Inference **Problem:** Prove the validity of the following argument. - Premise 1: If it is sunny, then I will go to the beach. ($p \rightarrow q$) - Premise 2: If I go to the beach, then I will swim. ($q \rightarrow r$) - Premise 3: It is sunny. ($p$) - Conclusion: I will swim. ($\therefore r$) | Step | Statement | Justification | |---|---|---| | 1 | $p \rightarrow q$ | Premise (given) | | 2 | $q \rightarrow r$ | Premise (given) | | 3 | $p \rightarrow r$ | Hypothetical Syllogism, lines 1 and 2 | | 4 | $p$ | Premise (given) | | 5 | $r$ | Modus Ponens, lines 3 and 4 | > [!NOTE] > **$\therefore$ Symbol:** The three-dot triangular symbol $\therefore$ means "therefore." It is *not* a logical connective — it is a meta-symbol that marks the conclusion of an argument. ### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Proof by Contradiction **Problem:** Prove that $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational. **Proof by contradiction:** 1. **Assume the negation of the conclusion:** Suppose $\sqrt{2}$ is rational. 2. **Express as a fraction:** Then there exist coprime integers $a, b$ (with $b \neq 0$) such that $\sqrt{2} = a/b$. 3. **Square both sides:** $2 = a^2 / b^2 \;\Rightarrow\; a^2 = 2b^2$. 4. **Divisibility argument:** $a^2$ is even $\Rightarrow a$ is even $\Rightarrow a = 2k$ for some integer $k$. 5. **Substitute:** $(2k)^2 = 2b^2 \;\Rightarrow\; 4k^2 = 2b^2 \;\Rightarrow\; b^2 = 2k^2 \;\Rightarrow\; b^2$ is even $\Rightarrow b$ is even. 6. **Contradiction:** Both $a$ and $b$ are even, contradicting the assumption that $a/b$ is in *lowest terms*. 7. **Conclusion:** The assumption must be false. Therefore, $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Quantifier Negation **Problem:** Express the negation of $\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$ in prenex normal form (no negation preceding a quantifier). $$ \begin{aligned} \neg(\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))) &\equiv \exists x \, \neg(P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)) & &\text{(Negation of Universal)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, \neg(\neg P(x) \lor Q(x)) & &\text{(Implication Elimination)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, (\neg\neg P(x) \land \neg Q(x)) & &\text{(De Morgan's Law)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, (P(x) \land \neg Q(x)) & &\text{(Double Negation)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap Question:** "Find the negation of $\forall x \, \exists y \, P(x,y)$." The correct answer is $\exists x \, \forall y \, \neg P(x,y)$ — students often forget to negate the *innermost* predicate $P(x,y)$ itself. ### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Proof Technique? | Goal | Best Technique | |---|---| | Prove a statement of the form "if $p$ then $q$" | Direct Proof or Contraposition | | Direct proof becomes too convoluted | Switch to Proof by Contradiction | | "For all integers $n$..." | Direct + Cases (even/odd) or Induction | | Disjunctive premises like $p \lor q$ | Proof by Cases | | "Show that $A \equiv B$" | Algebraic Equivalence or Truth Table | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Map of Mathematical Logic This diagram visualizes how the sub-topics of Mathematical Logic and Proofs interconnect. It serves as a **mental map** for KTU module-level study. ```mermaid graph TD rootA["Mathematical Logic and Proofs"] rootB["Propositional Logic"] rootC["Predicate Logic"] rootD["Methods of Proof"] rootA --> rootB rootA --> rootC rootA --> rootD rootB --> node1["Propositions and Connectives"] rootB --> node2["Truth Tables"] rootB --> node3["Logical Equivalence Laws"] rootB --> node4["Rules of Inference"] rootC --> node5["Predicates P of x"] rootC --> node6["Universal Quantifier FORALL"] rootC --> node7["Existential Quantifier EXISTS"] rootC --> node8["De Morgan for Quantifiers"] rootD --> node9["Direct Proof"] rootD --> node10["Proof by Contraposition"] rootD --> node11["Proof by Contradiction"] rootD --> node12["Proof by Cases"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> rootD node5 --> node6 node5 --> node7 node6 --> node8 node7 --> node8 node9 --> node10 node10 --> node11 node11 --> node12 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Logical Proof Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The diagram below models the **flow of operations** a mathematician (or an automated theorem prover) executes when constructing a valid logical argument. ```mermaid graph TD startA["Start: Premises Given"] block1["Step 1: Identify Logical Connectives"] block2["Step 2: Apply Implication Elimination if needed"] block3["Step 3: Choose Rule of Inference"] block4["Step 4: Construct Truth Table or Algebraic Proof"] block5["Step 5: Verify Every Row or Every Step"] block6["Step 6: State Conclusion with therefore symbol"] endA["End: Valid Argument Established"] startA --> block1 block1 --> block2 block2 --> block3 block3 --> block4 block4 --> block5 block5 --> block6 block6 --> endA block3 -.->|"Uses"| ruleSet["Modus Ponens
Modus Tollens
Hypothetical Syllogism
Disjunctive Syllogism
Resolution"] ruleSet -.-> block3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Quantifier Negation Flow This shows the **mechanical procedure** to negate a quantified statement — a frequent source of KTU errors. ```mermaid graph TD qstart["Quantified Statement S"] qstep1["Step 1: Flip the Quantifier"] qstep2["FORALL becomes EXISTS"] qstep3["EXISTS becomes FORALL"] qstep4["Step 2: Negate the Inner Predicate"] qstep5["Step 3: Push Negation Inside Using De Morgan"] qend["Result: Negated Statement NOT S"] qstart --> qstep1 qstep1 --> qstep2 qstep1 --> qstep3 qstep2 --> qstep4 qstep3 --> qstep4 qstep4 --> qstep5 qstep5 --> qend ``` ### 4.4 Visual Truth-Table Reference Card (Implication + Biconditional) For high-frequency KTU questions on $p \rightarrow q$ and $p \leftrightarrow q$, memorize the compact truth-table snippet: ```mermaid graph LR inpA["Input Combination"] inpA --> case1["TT: Implication T, Biconditional T"] inpA --> case2["TF: Implication F, Biconditional F"] inpA --> case3["FT: Implication T, Biconditional F"] inpA --> case4["FF: Implication T, Biconditional T"] case1 -.-> noteT["Implication is FALSE only in the TF row"] case2 -.-> noteT case3 -.-> noteT case4 -.-> noteT ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The single **only-False** row of $p \rightarrow q$ is the **T-F** combination. This single fact underwrites 80% of KTU implication questions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define a proposition. Determine whether each of the following is a proposition. Justify.* *(i) "10 + 7 = 17"*   *(ii) "Do you speak English?"*   *(iii) "The Earth orbits the Sun."* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** A proposition is a declarative sentence that has a definite truth value — either **True (T)** or **False (F)** — but not both and not neither. | Sentence | Proposition? | Reason | |---|---|---| | (i) "10 + 7 = 17" | **Yes — Proposition (False)** | Declarative and has a definite truth value (the arithmetic claim is wrong, but it is a meaningful statement). | | (ii) "Do you speak English?" | **No — Not a Proposition** | It is an interrogative (question), not a declarative sentence. | | (iii) "The Earth orbits the Sun." | **Yes — Proposition (True)** | Declarative factual claim with a definite truth value. | **(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for classification, 1 mark for justification.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *State and explain De Morgan's Laws in propositional logic. Write their truth-table verification.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **De Morgan's First Law:** $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$ **De Morgan's Second Law:** $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$ | $p$ | $q$ | $p \land q$ | $\neg(p \land q)$ | $\neg p \lor \neg q$ | $p \lor q$ | $\neg(p \lor q)$ | $\neg p \land \neg q$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | T | T | T | **F** | **F** | T | **F** | **F** | | T | F | F | **T** | **T** | T | **F** | **F** | | F | T | F | **T** | **T** | T | **F** | **F** | | F | F | F | **T** | **T** | F | **T** | **T** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Explanation (2 marks):** De Morgan's laws state that the negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of the negations, and vice versa. The truth-table columns $\neg(p \land q)$ and $\neg p \lor \neg q$ are identical, and $\neg(p \lor q)$ and $\neg p \land \neg q$ are identical — hence the equivalences hold. > **(Award: 1 mark for stating both laws, 2 marks for correct truth-table verification with identical columns.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) **INSTRUCTION (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.* --- #### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a)** Construct the truth table for the compound proposition: $$(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$$ Is the proposition a tautology, contradiction, or contingency? Justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Show using logical equivalences that $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Truth Table Construction (7 marks):** | $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $\neg p \lor q$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | T | T | F | T | T | **T** | | T | F | F | F | F | **T** | | F | T | T | T | T | **T** | | F | F | T | T | T | **T** | **Final column is T in every row — therefore the proposition is a TAUTOLOGY (always True).** **(Award: 5 marks for table, 1 mark for classification, 1 mark for justification.)** **(b) Algebraic Proof (7 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \neg(p \rightarrow q) &\equiv \neg(\neg p \lor q) & &\text{(Implication Elimination: 2 marks)} \\ &\equiv \neg(\neg p) \land \neg q & &\text{(De Morgan's Law: 2 marks)} \\ &\equiv p \land \neg q & &\text{(Double Negation Law: 2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **(Award: 1 mark for correct citation of laws at each step.)** --- #### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** State Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens. Test the validity of the following argument using these rules. - Premise 1: If $x$ is a prime number greater than 2, then $x$ is odd. ($p \rightarrow q$) - Premise 2: 7 is a prime number greater than 2. ($p$) - Premise 3: $x$ is odd. ($q$) - Conclusion: 7 is odd. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Find the negation of the following statement and simplify: $$\forall x \, (x > 0 \rightarrow \exists y \, (y^2 = x))$$ Express in a form with no negation preceding any quantifier or logical connective. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Rules of Inference (7 marks):** - **Modus Ponens:** From $p$ and $p \rightarrow q$, conclude $q$. *(2 marks for statement)* - **Modus Tollens:** From $\neg q$ and $p \rightarrow q$, conclude $\neg p$. *(2 marks for statement)* **Validity Test:** | Step | Statement | Justification | |---|---|---| | 1 | $p \rightarrow q$ | Premise (7 is prime > 2 implies odd) | | 2 | $p$ | Premise (7 is a prime > 2) | | 3 | $q$ | **Modus Ponens** applied to steps 1 and 2 (1 mark) | | 4 | $q \rightarrow r$ | Here $q$ is "odd" and $r$ is "7 is odd"; trivially true (1 mark) | | 5 | $r$ | Modus Ponens on steps 3 and 4 (1 mark) | **Therefore the argument is VALID — 7 is odd is a valid conclusion.** **(Award 7 marks.)** **(b) Negation with Quantifiers (7 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \neg(\forall x \, (x > 0 \rightarrow \exists y \, (y^2 = x))) &\equiv \exists x \, \neg(x > 0 \rightarrow \exists y \, (y^2 = x)) & &\text{(Flip universal to existential: 2 marks)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, \neg(\neg(x > 0) \lor \exists y \, (y^2 = x)) & &\text{(Implication Elimination: 1 mark)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, (\neg\neg(x > 0) \land \neg\exists y \, (y^2 = x)) & &\text{(De Morgan's Law: 1 mark)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, (x > 0 \land \forall y \, \neg(y^2 = x)) & &\text{(Double negation + flip existential: 2 marks)} \\ &\equiv \exists x \, (x > 0 \land \forall y \, (y^2 \neq x)) & &\text{(Final simplification: 1 mark)} \end{aligned} $$ **English reading:** "There exists a positive real number $x$ such that for all real numbers $y$, $y^2$ is not equal to $x$." This is the correct negation in simplest form. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Logic Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $\Leftrightarrow$ with $\equiv$.** $\Leftrightarrow$ is a *binary connective* (a proposition with truth value). $\equiv$ is a *meta-logical statement* (claims equality of two propositions). Mixing them costs 1–2 marks per question. > > 2. **Forgetting to negate the inner predicate.** "Negate $\forall x \, P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)$" — students flip the universal but forget to negate $P$ or $Q$. This costs 2–3 marks. > > 3. **Truth-table row count error.** Forgetting that $n$ atoms need exactly $2^n$ rows. A 3-atom table with 6 rows instead of 8 = full marks cancelled. > > 4. **Premise / conclusion direction in Modus Tollens.** Mixing up $\neg q$ with $\neg p$. Memorize: Modus Tollens takes you from $\neg q$ to $\neg p$ — *against* the arrow direction. > > 5. **Implication Elimination step omitted.** Writing $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv \neg p \lor q$ directly without showing the intermediate step. Always expand $p \rightarrow q$ to $\neg p \lor q$ *first*, then apply De Morgan. > > 6. **Domain of discourse not stated.** In quantified statements, KTU requires the domain to be mentioned. "$\forall x$" without domain = incomplete answer. > > 7. **Confusing $\rightarrow$ with $\Rightarrow$ in proofs.** The single arrow $\rightarrow$ is part of the formula; the double arrow $\Rightarrow$ is meta-language used between derivation steps. Examiners deduct for inconsistency. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2:** > > - [ ] **Proposition** = declarative sentence with a definite T/F value. > - [ ] **Five connectives** to memorize: $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$. > - [ ] **Implication** $p \rightarrow q$ is **False only** in the (T, F) case. All other cases are True. > - [ ] **Biconditional** $p \leftrightarrow q$ is True exactly when both $p$ and $q$ share the same truth value. > - [ ] **Tautology** = always True; **Contradiction** = always False; **Contingency** = mixed. > - [ ] **Implication Elimination:** $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$ — used in nearly every algebraic proof. > - [ ] **De Morgan's Laws** (both versions) — most heavily tested in KTU exams. > - [ ] **Contrapositive:** $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ — proves implication by indirect route. > - [ ] **Quantifier Negation rule:** Flip the quantifier ($\forall \leftrightarrow \exists$) AND negate the predicate. > - [ ] **Modus Ponens:** $p, \; p \rightarrow q \;\;\therefore\;\; q$ — most common inference rule. > - [ ] **Modus Tollens:** $\neg q, \; p \rightarrow q \;\;\therefore\;\; \neg p$ — second most common. > - [ ] **Hypothetical Syllogism:** $p \rightarrow q, \; q \rightarrow r \;\;\therefore\;\; p \rightarrow r$ — chains implications. > - [ ] **Proof by Contradiction:** Assume $\neg(\text{conclusion})$, derive $\bot$, conclude the original. > - [ ] **Truth-table rule:** $n$ atoms $\Rightarrow$ $2^n$ rows. > - [ ] **Precedence:** $\neg > \land > \lor > \rightarrow > \leftrightarrow$ — use parentheses to override. > - [ ] **$\therefore$ vs $\because$:** Three dots = therefore; inverted three dots = because.

topics.title: Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Propositional Logic — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propositional Logic (also called Propositional Calculus or Sentential Logic)** is the branch of mathematical logic that studies the logical relationships between **whole propositions** (declarative statements) and the ways in which they can be combined using **logical connectives** to form **compound propositions**, and the rules by which the truth values of the compound propositions are determined from the truth values of the component propositions.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this is the foundational tool used in:
- Digital circuit design (logic gates)
- Algorithm correctness proofs
- Database query languages (SQL WHERE clauses)
- Artificial intelligence and expert systems
- Software verification and formal methods

### 1.2 Proposition — The Atomic Unit

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Proposition):** A *proposition* is a declarative sentence that is either **definitely true (T)** or **definitely false (F)**, but **never both, and never neither**.

| Sentence | Proposition? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| "2 + 2 = 4" | ✅ Yes | Always True |
| "The Earth is flat" | ✅ Yes | Always False |
| "What time is it?" | ❌ No | It is a question, not a statement |
| "x + 1 = 5" | ❌ No | Truth value depends on unknown *x* (this is a *predicate*) |
| "This statement is false" | ❌ No | Self-referential paradox — neither strictly T nor F |
| "Close the door" | ❌ No | It is a command |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Light Switch Model

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of propositional logic like a **wall of light switches** in your house.
> - Each **switch** represents a single proposition — it has exactly two stable states: **ON (True)** or **OFF (False)**. It cannot flicker in between.
> - **Logical connectives** are like the *electrical wiring* between switches. An **AND** gate is a series circuit (both must be ON for the bulb to glow), an **OR** gate is a parallel circuit (either being ON lights the bulb), and a **NOT** gate is an inverter (reverses the state).
> - A **truth table** is the *user manual* that tells you, for every combination of switch positions, whether the final bulb is ON or OFF.
> - A **tautology** is a circuit that is *always ON* no matter how the switches are flipped — like a hallway light directly connected to the mains.
> - A **contradiction** is a circuit that is *always OFF* — like a bulb with a broken filament.

### 1.4 Atomic vs. Compound Propositions

- **Atomic (Simple) Proposition:** A proposition that cannot be broken down into smaller propositions. Represented by a single letter: $p, q, r, \ldots$
- **Compound Proposition:** A proposition formed by combining one or more atomic propositions using **logical connectives** (also called *logical operators*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Symbols for Logical Connectives (KTU Convention):**

| Symbol | Name | Pronounced As |
|---|---|---|
| $\lnot$ | Negation | "NOT $p$" |
| $\land$ | Conjunction | "$p$ AND $q$" |
| $\lor$ | Disjunction | "$p$ OR $q$" |
| $\oplus$ | Exclusive OR | "$p$ XOR $q$" |
| $\rightarrow$ | Implication / Conditional | "If $p$ then $q$" |
| $\leftrightarrow$ | Biconditional | "$p$ if and only if $q$" |

> **Other notations you may see:** $\bar{p}$ or $p'$ for $\lnot p$; $\cdot$ for $\land$; $+$ for $\lor$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Connectives — Complete Truth Tables

The truth value of a compound proposition is **uniquely determined** by the truth values of its component propositions. We capture this determinism in a **truth table**.

### 2.1.1 Negation ($\lnot p$)

> [!NOTE]
> The negation reverses the truth value. $\lnot p$ is the *logical complement* of $p$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
p & \lnot p \\
\hline
T & F \\
F & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- **Logic:** "It is NOT the case that $p$."
- **Example:** $p$: "It is raining." → $\lnot p$: "It is NOT raining."

### 2.1.2 Conjunction ($p \land q$)

> [!NOTE]
> True **only when both** $p$ AND $q$ are true. In *all other cases* it is false. This is the most restrictive operator.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \land q \\
\hline
T & T & T \\
T & F & F \\
F & T & F \\
F & F & F \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 2.1.3 Disjunction ($p \lor q$)

> [!NOTE]
> True **whenever at least one** of $p, q$ is true. False **only when both** are false. This is the *inclusive* OR.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \lor q \\
\hline
T & T & T \\
T & F & T \\
F & T & T \\
F & F & F \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 2.1.4 Exclusive OR ($p \oplus q$)

> [!NOTE]
> True **when exactly one** of $p, q$ is true. This is the *exclusive* OR — like choosing between two mutually exclusive options.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \oplus q \\
\hline
T & T & F \\
T & F & T \\
F & T & T \\
F & F & F \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 2.1.5 Implication / Conditional ($p \rightarrow q$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-examined** operator in KTU. It is read as "**If $p$ then $q$**" where $p$ is the *hypothesis* (antecedent / premise) and $q$ is the *conclusion* (consequent).

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \rightarrow q \\
\hline
T & T & T \\
T & F & F \\
F & T & T \\
F & F & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- The only **false** case is when $p$ is True and $q$ is False.
- When $p$ is False, the implication is **vacuously true** (no counterexample exists).
- **Real-world engineering analogy:** In a contract, "If you submit the report, you will be paid" — the contract is honored whether you submit or not, AS LONG AS if you DO submit, you are paid. The contract is violated ONLY if you submit but aren't paid.

### 2.1.6 Biconditional ($p \leftrightarrow q$)

> [!NOTE]
> True when $p$ and $q$ have the **same truth value** — both T or both F.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \leftrightarrow q \\
\hline
T & T & T \\
T & F & F \\
F & T & F \\
F & F & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- Equivalent to: $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$.

## 2.2 Logical Equivalence ($\equiv$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Two compound propositions $A$ and $B$ are *logically equivalent*, written $A \equiv B$, if and only if they have **identical truth tables** (i.e., the final columns match for **every** combination of truth values of the propositional variables).

### 2.2.1 The Core Equivalence Cheat Sheet

| Name | Equivalence (Law) |
|---|---|
| Identity | $p \land T \equiv p$ ; $\quad p \lor F \equiv p$ |
| Domination | $p \lor T \equiv T$ ; $\quad p \land F \equiv F$ |
| Idempotent | $p \land p \equiv p$ ; $\quad p \lor p \equiv p$ |
| Double Negation | $\lnot(\lnot p) \equiv p$ |
| Commutative | $p \land q \equiv q \land p$ ; $\quad p \lor q \equiv q \lor p$ |
| Associative | $(p \land q) \land r \equiv p \land (q \land r)$ ; $\quad (p \lor q) \lor r \equiv p \lor (q \lor r)$ |
| Distributive | $p \land (q \lor r) \equiv (p \land q) \lor (p \land r)$ ; $\quad p \lor (q \land r) \equiv (p \lor q) \land (p \lor r)$ |
| De Morgan's | $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$ ; $\quad \lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$ |
| Absorption | $p \lor (p \land q) \equiv p$ ; $\quad p \land (p \lor q) \equiv p$ |
| Negation | $p \lor \lnot p \equiv T$ ; $\quad p \land \lnot p \equiv F$ |
| Implication | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ |
| Contrapositive | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot q \rightarrow \lnot p$ |
| Biconditional | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$ |
| XOR | $p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Secret:** Memorize the two **implication identities** ($p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$) and the **contrapositive identity** — these alone cover ~40% of all proof-related questions in Module 2.

## 2.3 Converse, Inverse, Contrapositive

Given the conditional $p \rightarrow q$:

| Related Statement | Form | Truth Table Relation |
|---|---|---|
| **Converse** | $q \rightarrow p$ | **NOT equivalent** to original in general |
| **Inverse** | $\lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q$ | **NOT equivalent** to original; equivalent to converse |
| **Contrapositive** | $\lnot q \rightarrow \lnot p$ | **LOGICALLY EQUIVALENT** to $p \rightarrow q$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU exam trap: students write "since $p \rightarrow q$ is true, its converse $q \rightarrow p$ must also be true." This is **FALSE**. The converse and inverse are *not* logically equivalent to the original conditional. Only the **contrapositive** is equivalent.

## 2.4 Tautology, Contradiction, Contingency

- **Tautology:** A compound proposition that is **always True** regardless of truth values. (e.g., $p \lor \lnot p$)
- **Contradiction:** A compound proposition that is **always False**. (e.g., $p \land \lnot p$)
- **Contingency:** A compound proposition that is **sometimes True and sometimes False** (i.e., it is neither a tautology nor a contradiction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tautology Test:** If the final column of the truth table contains **only T**, it is a tautology.
> **Contradiction Test:** If the final column contains **only F**, it is a contradiction.

## 2.5 Logical Equivalence via Truth Table — The Standard KTU Method

> **Algorithm (Truth Table Equivalence Check):**
> 1. Identify all distinct propositional variables in $A$ and $B$.
> 2. List all $2^n$ rows of combinations (where $n$ is the number of variables).
> 3. Compute the truth value of $A$ in each row (build helper columns if needed).
> 4. Compute the truth value of $B$ in each row.
> 5. Compare column by column. If they match in **every** row, $A \equiv B$. Otherwise, they are not equivalent.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Summary Table

| Concept | Symbolic Statement | Critical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Negation | $\lnot p$ | Complement |
| Conjunction | $p \land q$ | All conditions must hold |
| Disjunction | $p \lor q$ | At least one must hold |
| Exclusive OR | $p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q)$ | Exactly one |
| Implication Rewrite | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ | Convert to DNF |
| Biconditional Rewrite | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (\lnot p \lor q) \land (\lnot q \lor p)$ | Convert to CNF |
| De Morgan's | $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$ | Push negation inward |
| Contrapositive | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot q \rightarrow \lnot p$ | For proofs |
| Exportation | $(p \land q) \rightarrow r \equiv p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow r)$ | Nested conditionals |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Propositional Logic |
|---|---|
| **Digital Electronics** | Each connective corresponds to a logic gate: $\lnot$ = NOT, $\land$ = AND, $\lor$ = OR, $\rightarrow$ = IMPLIES (NAND/OR combo), $\leftrightarrow$ = XNOR, $\oplus$ = XOR |
| **Computer Architecture** | ALU operations, comparator circuits, multiplexers |
| **Database Systems** | WHERE clauses in SQL; query optimization using Boolean algebra |
| **Software Engineering** | Pre/post-conditions in contracts, model checking |
| **Cryptography** | Boolean functions in block ciphers (DES, AES S-boxes) |
| **AI / Expert Systems** | Rule-based reasoning: IF (antecedent) THEN (action) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Showing $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$

**Objective:** Prove that the conditional $p \rightarrow q$ is logically equivalent to $\lnot p \lor q$ by constructing the full truth table.

| Step | Justification |
|---|---|
| Step 1: Identify variables | There are **2 variables**: $p$ and $q$. |
| Step 2: List $2^2 = 4$ rows | (T,T), (T,F), (F,T), (F,F) |
| Step 3: Compute $p \rightarrow q$ for each row using the implication rule | The only FALSE case is T,F. |
| Step 4: Compute $\lnot p$ for each row | Invert column $p$. |
| Step 5: Compute $\lnot p \lor q$ for each row using disjunction rule | TRUE if either operand is TRUE. |
| Step 6: Compare final columns | They must be identical. |

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \rightarrow q & \lnot p & \lnot p \lor q \\
\hline
T & T & T & F & T \\
T & F & F & F & F \\
F & T & T & T & T \\
F & F & T & T & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Column 3 (Implication):} \quad & p \rightarrow q \\
\text{Column 5 (Disjunction):} \quad & \lnot p \lor q \\
\text{Comparison: } \quad & \text{Column 3} \equiv \text{Column 5} \quad \text{for all rows.} \\
\therefore \quad & p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: De Morgan's Law — $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$

**Strategy:** Build the full truth table for both sides and show the final columns are identical.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \land q & \lnot(p \land q) & \lnot p & \lnot q & \lnot p \lor \lnot q \\
\hline
T & T & T & F & F & F & F \\
T & F & F & T & F & T & T \\
F & T & F & T & T & F & T \\
F & F & F & T & T & T & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute } p \land q: \text{ AND of columns 1 and 2.} \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute } \lnot(p \land q): \text{ Invert column 3.} \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute } \lnot p \text{ and } \lnot q: \text{ Invert columns 1 and 2 separately.} \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compute } \lnot p \lor \lnot q: \text{ OR of columns 5 and 6.} \\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Compare column 4 with column 7.} \\
& \text{Row 1: F = F ✅} \\
& \text{Row 2: T = T ✅} \\
& \text{Row 3: T = T ✅} \\
& \text{Row 4: T = T ✅} \\
\therefore \quad & \lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Showing the Inverse is NOT Equivalent to the Original

**Objective:** Construct a **counterexample** to show $p \rightarrow q \not\equiv \lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q$.

> **Counterexample:** Let $p$: "It is a dog", $q$: "It is a mammal."
> - $p \rightarrow q$: "If it is a dog, then it is a mammal." — **TRUE** (all dogs are mammals).
> - $\lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q$: "If it is NOT a dog, then it is NOT a mammal." — **FALSE** (a cat is not a dog but IS a mammal).

Truth-table confirmation:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \rightarrow q & \lnot p & \lnot q & \lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q \\
\hline
T & T & T & F & F & T \\
T & F & F & F & T & T \\
F & T & T & T & F & F \\
F & F & T & T & T & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Column 3: } p \rightarrow q \text{ has values T, F, T, T.} \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Column 6: } \lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q \text{ has values T, T, F, T.} \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Row 2: Column 3 is F, but Column 6 is T.} \\
\therefore \quad & \text{They differ.} \quad p \rightarrow q \not\equiv \lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Showing $p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q)$

| Step | Reasoning |
|---|---|
| Step 1 | Build a truth table with 4 rows for variables $p, q$. |
| Step 2 | Compute $p \oplus q$ using XOR rule (T only when operands differ). |
| Step 3 | Compute the RHS: first $p \lor q$, then $p \land q$, then $\lnot(p \land q)$, then AND the two. |
| Step 4 | Compare the final two columns. |

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \oplus q & p \lor q & p \land q & \lnot(p \land q) & (p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q) \\
\hline
T & T & F & T & T & F & F \\
T & F & T & T & F & T & T \\
F & T & T & T & F & T & T \\
F & F & F & F & F & T & F \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Comparison:} \quad & \text{Column 3 vs Column 7.} \\
& \text{Row 1: F = F ✅} \quad \text{Row 2: T = T ✅} \\
& \text{Row 3: T = T ✅} \quad \text{Row 4: F = F ✅} \\
\therefore \quad & p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \lnot(p \land q) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Proving $(p \rightarrow q) \land (p \rightarrow r) \equiv p \rightarrow (q \land r)$

> This identity is **critical** in KTU exam questions on simplifying compound propositions.

**LHS Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p \rightarrow q) \land (p \rightarrow r) &\equiv (\lnot p \lor q) \land (\lnot p \lor r) \\
&\equiv \lnot p \lor (q \land r) \quad \text{(Distributive Law)} \\
&\equiv p \rightarrow (q \land r) \quad \text{(Implication Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Each step's justification:**
- Line 1: $p \rightarrow q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ applied to both sub-conditionals.
- Line 2: Distributive law $A \lor (B \land C) \equiv (A \lor B) \land (A \lor C)$ reversed; here $A = \lnot p$.
- Line 3: Reverse of the implication law.

$$
\therefore \quad (p \rightarrow q) \land (p \rightarrow r) \equiv p \rightarrow (q \land r) \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operator Precedence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU, the order of operations in compound propositions (highest precedence first) is:

$$
\text{1. Parentheses ( )} \quad > \quad \text{2. Negation } (\lnot) \quad > \quad \text{3. Conjunction } (\land) \quad > \quad \text{4. Disjunction } (\lor) \quad > \quad \text{5. Implication } (\rightarrow) \quad > \quad \text{6. Biconditional } (\leftrightarrow)
$$

**Example:** The expression $\lnot p \land q \rightarrow r$ parses as $(((\lnot p) \land q) \rightarrow r)$.

## 3.7 Worked Example: Determine Whether $(p \rightarrow q) \lor (\lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q)$ is a Tautology

**Step 1:** There are 2 variables; build 4-row truth table.

**Step 2:** Compute intermediate columns.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
p & q & p \rightarrow q & \lnot p & \lnot q & \lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q & (p \rightarrow q) \lor (\lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q) \\
\hline
T & T & T & F & F & T & T \\
T & F & F & F & T & T & T \\
F & T & T & T & F & F & T \\
F & F & T & T & T & T & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3:** The final column is **all T**. Hence the proposition is a **TAUTOLOGY**.

$$
\therefore (p \rightarrow q) \lor (\lnot p \rightarrow \lnot q) \equiv T \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.8 Worked Example: Negate and Simplify $\lnot(\lnot(p \land q) \lor r)$

> [!TIP]
> **Method:** Apply De Morgan's Law, then Double Negation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lnot(\lnot(p \land q) \lor r) &\equiv \lnot(\lnot(p \land q)) \land \lnot r & & \text{De Morgan's} \\
&\equiv (p \land q) \land \lnot r & & \text{Double Negation} \\
&\equiv p \land q \land \lnot r & & \text{Associativity}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Each step's justification:**
- **Line 1:** De Morgan's Law: $\lnot(A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$. Here $A = \lnot(p \land q)$ and $B = r$.
- **Line 2:** Double Negation: $\lnot(\lnot X) \equiv X$, applied to $X = p \land q$.
- **Line 3:** Associativity of $\land$ allows re-grouping.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture of Propositional Logic

The following block-level architecture maps the conceptual structure of Propositional Logic as a complete system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC SYSTEM] --> B[Atomic Propositions]
    A --> C[Logical Connectives]
    A --> D[Compound Propositions]
    A --> E[Meta-Properties]
    
    B --> B1[Proposition p: True or False]
    B --> B2[Proposition q: True or False]
    B --> B3[Proposition r: True or False]
    
    C --> C1[Negation: NOT]
    C --> C2[Conjunction: AND]
    C --> C3[Disjunction: OR]
    C --> C4[Implication: IF THEN]
    C --> C5[Biconditional: IFF]
    C --> C6[XOR: Exclusive OR]
    
    D --> D1[Built using Connectives]
    D --> D2[Evaluated by Truth Table]
    D --> D3[Subject to Equivalence Laws]
    
    E --> E1[Tautology: Always True]
    E --> E2[Contradiction: Always False]
    E --> E3[Contingency: Mixed Truth]
    E --> E4[Logical Equivalence]
    E --> E5[Contradiction and Contrapositive]
```

## 4.2 Truth Table Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START: Given Compound Proposition] --> S2[Identify all variables: n]
    S2 --> S3[Compute 2 to the power n rows]
    S3 --> S4[Enumerate all T/F combinations]
    S4 --> S5[Build helper columns for sub-expressions]
    S5 --> S6[Apply connective rules step by step]
    S6 --> S7[Compute final output column]
    S7 --> S8{Analyze Final Column}
    S8 -- All T --> S9[Result: TAUTOLOGY]
    S8 -- All F --> S10[Result: CONTRADICTION]
    S8 -- Mixed --> S11[Result: CONTINGENCY]
    S8 -- Compare with other proposition --> S12[Check EQUIVALENCE]
```

## 4.3 The Family Tree of an Implication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Original Conditional: p implies q] --> Q[Converse: q implies p]
    P --> R[Inverse: NOT p implies NOT q]
    P --> S[Contrapositive: NOT q implies NOT p]
    
    Q -.->|EQUIVALENT| R
    P ==>|EQUIVALENT| S
    Q -.->|NOT EQUIVALENT| P
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **double-line arrow** (==>) indicates **logical equivalence**.
- The **dashed arrow** (-.->) indicates that the converse and inverse are equivalent to **each other** but **not** to the original.

## 4.4 De Morgan's Law — Structural Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph D1[De Morgan Law 1]
        DM1A[NOT p AND q] --> DM1B[NOT p OR NOT q]
    end
    
    subgraph D2[De Morgan Law 2]
        DM2A[NOT p OR q] --> DM2B[NOT p AND NOT q]
    end
    
    DM1A -->|EQUIVALENT| DM1B
    DM2A -->|EQUIVALENT| DM2B
```

## 4.5 Logic Gate Correspondence (Engineering Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LG[LOGIC GATE MAPPING]
        L1[Propositional Operator] --> L2[Digital Logic Gate]
    end
    
    P1[NOT] --> G1[NOT Gate: Inverter]
    P2[AND] --> G2[AND Gate]
    P3[OR] --> G3[OR Gate]
    P4[XOR] --> G4[XOR Gate]
    P5[IF THEN] --> G5[OR gate with inverted input]
    P6[IFF] --> G6[XNOR Gate]
```
